title: browsertrix: import a user's WACZ files from Browsertrix
description:
Add a 'rustyweb browsertrix' subcommand that authenticates to a Browsertrix instance (Webrecorder's hosted crawling service, which produces the WACZ files rustyweb serves), lists a user's archived items, downloads each WACZ into the home directory's archive folder, and indexes it as a local (File) source.

Grounded in the Browsertrix REST API (from its OpenAPI):
- Auth: POST /api/auth/jwt/login (form username/password) -> JWT bearer token.
- Whoami/orgs: GET /api/users/me, GET /api/orgs (paginated).
- List items: GET /api/orgs/{oid}/all-crawls (crawls + uploads), paginated.
- WACZ: GET /api/orgs/{oid}/all-crawls/{id}/download (authenticated stream) for downloading; GET /api/orgs/{oid}/crawls/{id}/replay.json for presigned resource URLs. Collections: /api/orgs/{oid}/collections/{id}/download.

DESIGN
- Command: rustyweb browsertrix [--host URL] [--org SLUG] [--home DIR] [filters].
- Uses --home like the other subcommands (default '.'); WACZs are written under <home>/archive and the index under <home>/index.
- Host default https://app.browsertrix.com; --host for self-hosted.
- Credentials from env (BROWSERTRIX_USER / BROWSERTRIX_PASSWORD) rather than flags, so passwords do not appear in the process list. (Optionally a token.)
- Flow: login -> resolve org (--org or the sole org) -> list all-crawls (paginated) -> for each item, download its WACZ into <home>/archive/<name>.wacz, then index that local file.
- ALWAYS DOWNLOAD (no --no-download for now): stream the authenticated /download endpoint straight into <home>/archive and index as a File source, so it is durable and replayable long-term. This matches rustyweb's rule that local WACZs live under <home>/archive (index stores them home-relative and portable). Browsertrix presigned/replay.json URLs are short-lived, so indexing them as remote URL sources would break replay after expiry - hence we download. (A remote/no-download mode was considered and deferred: rustyweb-15z.4, closed.)
- Provenance + incremental: record the Browsertrix item id on the collection so re-runs skip already-indexed items unless --force.
- Reuse ureq + serde_json; no new heavy deps. Test against a mock HTTP server serving canned JSON, not the live service.

This is a way to close the loop with Webrecorder, whose Browsertrix creates the WACZ files and whose ReplayWeb.page does the replay.
notes: When implemented, imported WACZs should be assigned to a Collection per the two-level model in rustyweb-collections-model-5co, and downloaded into <home>/archive (already reflected in this epics design).

comments.author: edsu
comments.text:
Priority/framing (maintainer): the Browsertrix importer is strategically key -- it's the "index your own crawls" on-ramp for adoption, and promoting Webrecorder / Browsertrix tooling is an explicit goal. Treat it as a first-class track once the hosted SUCHO demo path is up (ingest + deploy), not a side project.

Streaming decision to make before building 15z.1-.3: revisit 15z.3 ("always download"). The maintainer prefers indexing WACZs where they are (streaming, no download) -- it keeps the on-disk footprint index-only, which is ideal for a self-hoster. Streaming is now proven (rustyweb-streaming-index-r0n). So a Browsertrix importer that resolves an org's WACZ resource URLs (per the browsertrix public API) and STREAMS them when range + CORS allow is more coherent than always-download. Keep download as the fallback (private or non-range-capable hosts). Decide streaming-first vs always-download up front, since it shapes 15z.1/.2/.3.

comments.author: edsu
comments.text: Related: the finding-aid provenance epic (rustyweb-findingaid-aoc3) is folded into this Browsertrix work. It adds EAD-style curatorial fields (scope/creator/dates/rights/subjects/provenance, Markdown notes) to the Collection model + a CollectionFields update struct; task 15z.6 (map Browsertrix item metadata -> collection metadata) is the population path and now depends on findingaid.1 (the model + merge struct). Build the model/CLI/UI first, then map Browsertrix metadata onto it.

comments.author: edsu
comments.text:
Resolved the streaming-vs-download question flagged in the 2026-07-18 comment (maintainer): the importer targets the AUTHENTICATED API (JWT -> a user's own crawls; the 'index your own crawls' on-ramp), and supports BOTH ingest modes, with STREAMING-FIRST as the priority (it's the path that handles the signed/presigned WACZ URLs).

15z.1 (API client) landed on branch browsertrix-api-client: crates/rustyweb-lib/src/browsertrix.rs. A Transport-trait-abstracted client (mirrors http_range::RangeFetch) with: login (JWT), me(), orgs(), items() (paginated all-crawls), resources() (replay.json -> presigned range-capable paths = the streaming target), and download()/download_url() (authenticated /download stream = the durable fallback). 8 unit tests against canned JSON via a FakeTransport (no live server / no mock-HTTP dep); the mock-server end-to-end test stays with 15z.7. Deferred for a follow-up/.3: wiring the client's GET/POST through http_range's retry/backoff (transient 429/503 politeness) for long paginated ingests.

comments.author: edsu
comments.text: Import controls added to .2/.3 (branch browsertrix-api-client): (a) selection — --collection <id> / --crawl <id> to import a specific Browsertrix collection or item (server-side all-crawls filters), with --into <name> as the rustyweb target collection (renamed from --collection); (b) QA filtering. Decision (maintainer): default to REVIEWED-ONLY — only import crawls a reviewer has QA'd in Browsertrix (reviewStatus set). --include-unreviewed imports everything; --min-review <1-5> sets a rating threshold; a single named --crawl is always included. Rationale: for a published/shared instance you want vetted content by default; the skipped-unreviewed count is logged with a hint so an all-unreviewed account isn't a silent no-op.

title: Browsertrix API client (auth, orgs, list items, resources)
description: Small module using ureq + serde_json: POST /api/auth/jwt/login for a JWT; GET /api/users/me and /api/orgs to resolve orgs; GET /api/orgs/{oid}/all-crawls (paginated) to list items; helpers to get a WACZ download stream (/all-crawls/{id}/download) and presigned resources (replay.json). Configurable host; bearer auth; handle pagination and API errors.

title: rustyweb browsertrix CLI subcommand + credentials/org selection
description: Add the subcommand with --host (default https://app.browsertrix.com), --org (slug/id; default the sole org), --home (default '.', like the other subcommands; WACZs go under <home>/archive and the index under <home>/index), and basic filters. Always downloads (no --no-download flag for now). Read username/password from env (BROWSERTRIX_USER/BROWSERTRIX_PASSWORD) to keep secrets out of argv; support a token too.

title: Download-and-index flow (always download)
description: For each selected item, stream the authenticated /download endpoint directly into <home>/archive/<name>.wacz (creating archive/ if needed), then index that local file through the existing pipeline (index_path/index_one) so it is registered as a File source stored relative to home. This satisfies rustyweb's rule that local WACZs live under <home>/archive. Choose a stable, filesystem-safe filename from the item (e.g. the crawl/item name or id); set the collection name/metadata from the item. Handle re-runs idempotently (overwrite/skip per the provenance task rustyweb-15z.5).

title: Remote (--no-download) mode via presigned replay.json URLs
description: With --no-download, fetch replay.json and index the presigned WACZ URL(s) as a URL source. Document clearly that Browsertrix presigned URLs are short-lived, so replay stops working after expiry; recommend --download for durable libraries.
close_reason: Deferred: start by always downloading. Presigned Browsertrix URLs are short-lived so the no-download/remote-URL mode is not durable; revisit later if needed.

title: Provenance + incremental sync (skip already-indexed)
description: Record the Browsertrix item id (and instance host) on the Collection (small optional provenance field). On re-run, skip items already indexed unless --force, so syncing a large account is cheap. Consider content hash as a secondary check.

title: Map Browsertrix item metadata to collection metadata
description: Map the item's name/description/created/tags to the collection's name/description/crawl date so imported collections show good metadata on the homepage and collection page.

title: Docs + tests (mock Browsertrix API)
description: Document the browsertrix command (host, org, credentials via env, download vs no-download, expiry caveat) in README/DESIGN. Test the API client and flow against a local HTTP mock serving canned login/orgs/all-crawls JSON and WACZ bytes; do not hit the live service.

title: Index nested (multi-)WACZ files
description:
WACZ files can nest: a multi-WACZ is a ZIP whose datapackage.json lists other .wacz files (conventionally under data/) as resources, with no top-level archive/ WARCs. This is exactly what Browsertrix's public collection /download endpoint returns for any collection with >1 crawl.

rustyweb's indexer currently assumes a flat single-level WACZ and would SILENTLY index a multi-WACZ as empty (no error):
- wacz.rs iter_warc_paths (~L257) only yields entries under archive/ ending in .warc.gz/.warc; a multi-WACZ has none.
- find_cdx_entry/search_cdx read the single top-level CDX; read_datapackage reads the single top-level datapackage.json.
Replay is unaffected: wabac.js/ReplayWeb.page already resolves nested WACZs via range reads. The gap is indexing-only.

Scope:
- Detect the multi-WACZ case in wacz.rs (datapackage resources are .wacz, or no archive/ WARCs but .wacz entries exist).
- Recurse into each nested .wacz entry (extract to temp) to pull its WARCs, CDX, and pages for full-text + CDX indexing.
- Recommended approach A (recurse and flatten): keep one Wacz manifest entry per outer file; index all nested content. Alternative B (explode nested WACZs into Collection members) deferred; member file-resolution is trickier since members are ZIP entries, not files.
- Add a multi-WACZ fixture + test. Real fixture available: Browsertrix public /download for a multi-crawl collection, e.g. climate-economic-justice-screening-tool (3 crawls).
- Note in code/docs that replay already handles nesting.

Prerequisite for the simpler Browsertrix import path (one combined /download per collection instead of enumerating + downloading each crawl WACZ).

title: Index PDF text content
description: Extract and index text from application/pdf response records in WACZ files. Today only text/html is indexed, so archived PDFs are invisible to search. Start with the pure-Rust pdf-extract crate (born-digital PDFs; no OCR).
close_reason: PDF text indexing shipped

description: Add the pdf-extract crate and a helper that returns extracted text (and a title if available) from PDF bytes. Pure-Rust, no native deps; born-digital PDFs only (no OCR).
close_reason: PDF text extraction + indexing shipped; 55 tests pass

title: Index application/pdf response records
description: In index.rs, detect response records with mimetype application/pdf and index the extracted text as a page document (URL = the PDF's URL; title from PDF metadata or the URL basename).
close_reason: PDF text extraction + indexing shipped; 55 tests pass

title: Fail gracefully on unparseable PDFs
description: PDF extraction can fail or hang on malformed/encrypted/scanned files. Catch errors, log at debug/warn, and skip the record — never fail the whole index run because of one bad PDF.
close_reason: PDF text extraction + indexing shipped; 55 tests pass

title: PDF fixture and test
description: Add a small WACZ (or WARC) fixture containing a born-digital PDF and assert its text is searchable after indexing.
close_reason: PDF text extraction + indexing shipped; 55 tests pass

title: Index browser-rendered text (urn:text records)
description: Browsertrix stores fully rendered (post-JavaScript) page text as urn:text:<url> WARC records. We currently index only raw text/html, so JS-heavy pages (SPAs like the storymaps fixture) are barely searchable — only their <title>. Index the urn:text payload, mapped back to the real URL, to dramatically improve search over rendered content, with no HTML scraping.
close_reason: urn:text rendered text indexing shipped

title: Detect and parse urn:text: records; map back to real URL
description: In index.rs, recognize response records whose WARC-Target-URI starts with 'urn:text:'. Strip the prefix to recover the real page URL. The payload is plain UTF-8 rendered page text.
close_reason: urn:text rendered text now indexed; per-URL merge; 52 tests pass

title: Restructure indexing to one page doc per URL, best body wins
description: index_warc_for_search currently streams records and indexes each HTML response independently. Restructure to collect per-URL so a page gets a single Tantivy doc: prefer urn:text payload for the body, fall back to HTML-scraped text, and take the title from the HTML <title> when available. Avoid double-indexing the same URL.
close_reason: urn:text rendered text now indexed; per-URL merge; 52 tests pass

title: Tests: rendered SPA text becomes searchable
description: Using a.wacz (storymaps SPA), assert that a distinctive phrase from the rendered body — not just the title — is searchable after indexing. Guards the urn:text path and the before/after search-quality win.
close_reason: urn:text rendered text now indexed; per-URL merge; 52 tests pass

title: WACZ metadata and homepage
description: Extract datapackage.json from each indexed WACZ during rustyweb index. Store title, description, crawl date, and seed pages in collections.json. Surface this metadata on the homepage so users can see what each collection contains before searching or replaying.
close_reason: All child tasks complete; WACZ-direct redesign shipped and verified

title: Add WaczMetadata struct and read_datapackage() to wacz.rs
description: Open WACZ ZIP, read and parse datapackage.json. Extract title, description, created (ISO 8601), and seed pages array. Return WaczMetadata struct. Handle missing fields gracefully.
close_reason: Implemented in WACZ-direct redesign; 46 tests pass, browser replay verified

title: Store metadata fields in collections.json
description: Add description, crawl_date, and seed_pages fields to the Collection struct in collections.rs. Remove record_count (no CDX store). Call read_datapackage() during rustyweb index and populate these fields.
close_reason: Implemented in WACZ-direct redesign; 46 tests pass, browser replay verified

title: Surface metadata on homepage
description: Update the homepage handler in server.rs to render each collection's description, crawl_date, and seed_pages (as clickable links to the replay viewer) alongside the existing name/path table.
close_reason: Implemented in WACZ-direct redesign; 46 tests pass, browser replay verified

title: Atomic reindex: build into temp dir and swap, so a failed rebuild never touches the working index
description:
reindex currently rebuilds the full-text index destructively and in place: it deletes index/full_text up front, then recreates it from the manifest sources. If the rebuild fails partway (a remote source down, a crash, disk full, process killed), you are left with a partial index — worse than before you started. The resilient-reindex work (rustyweb-reindex-resilient-aev) softened this: a source that fails after retries is now skipped with a warning and reindex still commits a usable-but-incomplete index and exits non-zero. But the working index is still mutated in place, so a hard failure (crash/kill mid-rebuild, not a per-source error) can still leave it degraded.

Make reindex atomic: build the new index into a sibling temp dir (e.g. index/full_text.new) and swap it in with an atomic rename only once the rebuild fully succeeds. On any failure, the existing index/full_text is left untouched — you are never worse off than before the reindex.

Notes / considerations:
- No copy of the old index is needed. reindex already builds the new full-text index from scratch from the sources, so "build into temp" just means writing that fresh build to a sibling path instead of overwriting in place. Cost is transient extra disk: old + new full_text coexist until the swap (~2x full-text index size for the duration).
- Swap: build into <index>/full_text.new, then rename old -> full_text.old (or remove), rename new -> full_text. rename on the same filesystem is atomic. Clean up any stale .new/.old dirs from a previous interrupted run at start.
- Interaction with skip-on-failure: decide the policy when some sources were skipped. Options: still swap in the partial index (current behavior: usable but incomplete) but from the temp dir, OR only swap when 0 sources were skipped and otherwise keep the old index + exit non-zero. Leaning toward: swap in the partial rebuild (so a mostly-good rebuild still lands) but keep exiting non-zero — preserves today's semantics while removing the mid-rebuild-crash risk. Confirm with maintainer.
- Manifest (collections.json / waczs.json) is separate and tiny; keep its save ordered so it stays consistent with whichever index is live after the swap.
- Tests: simulate a failure after the temp index is partly built and assert the original index is still intact and searchable; assert a successful reindex swaps cleanly and leaves no .new/.old dirs.

Follow-up to rustyweb-reindex-resilient-aev (now closed). Ties to the shareable/self-serve goal (a reindex on someone else's box shouldn't be able to destroy their working index).

title: Web archive authenticity (WACZ signatures)
description: Future work on verifiable authenticity, distinct from provenance. A signed WACZ carries a datapackage-digest.json with a hash + cryptographic signature of datapackage.json (webrecorder WACZ auth spec). Two stages: (a) detect and display presence of a signature (no crypto deps); (b) verify the signature against the datapackage (public-key or certificate-signed; needs crypto). Complements the existing sha256 fixity (verify command). Moved out of the provenance epic (was rustyweb-provenance-imd.4) so provenance can wrap up.

title: Detect and display WACZ signature
description: Detect datapackage-digest.json in the WACZ and show a signed/verified status on the collection page alongside the sha256 fixity. Stage (a): detect + display presence (no crypto). Full cryptographic verification is a follow-on stage (b).

title: search-url CLI command
description: Replace the CDX-store-backed rustyweb lookup with a new rustyweb search-url command that reads WACZ CDX files on-the-fly. Opens each indexed WACZ, reads indexes/index.cdx.gz, and prints matching records grouped by collection.

description: Open WACZ ZIP, read and decompress indexes/index.cdx.gz, parse CDXJ lines (surt timestamp {json}). Match by exact URL or SURT prefix. Return Vec<CdxjRecord> with url, timestamp, status, mime, filename, offset, length fields.

title: Two-level collections (Collection groups WACZs)
description: Introduce a first-class curated Collection that groups many WACZ/crawl members, replacing the current one-WACZ-is-one-collection model. A Collection carries curatorial provenance (name, description, intent/scope) and becomes the primary browse and facet unit; each member WACZ keeps its technical provenance (see the provenance epic). This scales rustyweb from an individual archive to an institutional one (e.g. reorganizing TBs of WARC) and is the structural fix for the long collection list. See the Discovery, Provenance and Collections section of DESIGN.md.
close_reason: Two-level collections complete: data model + migration (.1), collection facet in search (.2), grouping + metadata CLI (.3), browse UI (.4).

title: Collection group data model + migration
description: Introduce a Collection (group) entity distinct from a WACZ member; define the manifest layout (group id/name/description/curatorial fields plus members referencing a group). Migration: existing flat collections.json entries become single-member collections (or an unfiled group); keep older manifests readable.
close_reason: Two-level data model landed: renamed the per-WACZ record to Wacz (member), added a first-class Collection (group) with its own metadata (id/name/description/created/curator), and a two-file Manifest (collections.json=groups + waczs.json=members) with migration from the old flat collections.json. Behavior preserved: each WACZ is a singleton collection (group id == WACZ id), so routes/links/UI are unchanged. All callers updated; 68 lib + 30 integration tests green.

title: Index a curated-collection field
description: Add a collection (group) field to page and collection documents in the Tantivy schema (search.rs build_schema and index_page) so search can filter and facet by curated collection.
close_reason: Added a 'collection' field (curated collection id) to page and collection docs, enabling 'collection:<slug>' filtering. SearchResult carries it; results link 'in <collection>' to /collection/{id}; API includes it; search tips document it. Verified end-to-end (collection:demo vs collection:other). Tests green.

title: CLI to assign or group WACZs into collections
description: Assign a WACZ to a collection at index time (e.g. --collection NAME) and/or add a collection subcommand to group existing WACZs. Reindex-friendly.
close_reason: Added 'index --collection NAME' to group WACZs (id = slug of name, created if new; membership preserved on reindex) and a 'collection set/list' subcommand for curatorial metadata (description/curator). Renamed the collection_id() helper to wacz_id() and standardized on 'collection' vocabulary (no 'group'). Tests + CLI e2e green.

title: Browse by collection (homepage + drill-down)
description: Homepage lists Collections instead of raw WACZs; the collection page lists member crawls with per-crawl provenance and drills down. Resolves the long-list navigation problem.
close_reason: Browse UI: homepage lists Collections (name, WACZ count, aggregate software + capture range, description); /collection/{id} shows collection metadata (curator/created/aggregates) + member WACZ list; new /wacz/{id} detail page (provenance panel, seeds, replay) with a breadcrumb back to its collection; search results link 'in <wacz>' to /wacz/{id}. Tests updated + e2e verified.

title: Representative image/thumbnail for collection cards, collection detail, and crawl detail (from WACZ screenshots)
description:
Idea (maintainer): use a representative image to make collection cards and detail pages (and crawl detail pages) more visual -- rather than the current text-only cards/lists. This matters for the shareable/adoption story and for the blog post's screenshots.

Source of the image: Browsertrix WACZs often contain page screenshots, stored as WARC resource records (target URIs like `urn:view:<url>`, `urn:thumbnail:<url>`, `urn:screenshot:<url>`, image/png|jpeg payloads). We already consume a sibling record type -- Browsertrix's rendered-text `urn:text:` records (index.rs:674+, RenderedText) -- so this extends that same pattern to image records. Verify the exact URN scheme / content types against a real Browsertrix WACZ before committing to field names.

Scope (v1, keep it small):
- Pick ONE representative image per crawl (WACZ): e.g. the thumbnail/screenshot of the seed page (first page in pages.jsonl), preferring `urn:thumbnail:` if present (small) else downsizing a larger screenshot. Don't ingest all screenshots.
- Store just enough to serve it: record the chosen image's location (which inner WARC + offset/length, consistent with CDX-guided extraction) on the crawl's manifest entry, OR extract+cache the bytes under <home>/index (decide; caching avoids a range fetch on every card render but costs disk). Lean toward a small cached thumbnail (e.g. index/thumbs/{wacz_id}.jpg) so serving is cheap and works for local + remote sources uniformly.
- Serve it: a route like /thumb/{wacz_id} (or /crawl/{id}/thumb) returning the cached image with caching headers, mirroring serve_embedded_asset's ETag/Cache-Control.
- Display it: collection card (homepage), collection detail (a representative image, likely from one member crawl), and crawl detail. Collection-level image = pick one member's thumbnail (e.g. first/most-recent member) for v1.
- Graceful fallback: many WACZs (esp. non-Browsertrix, e.g. py-wacz) have NO screenshots -- must degrade cleanly to today's text-only card/detail. No broken image boxes.

Considerations:
- Only extract during indexing (index_one), alongside the existing record pass, to avoid a second scan. Optional flag to disable (privacy/size).
- Size/perf: thumbnails should be small (cap dimensions/bytes); consider re-encoding to JPEG/WebP. Adds an image dep (e.g. `image` crate) -- weigh against the single-binary/pure-Rust ethos (image is pure Rust; OK).
- Privacy/appropriateness: a screenshot may show sensitive content; keep it opt-outable and only use the seed/representative page.

Relates to the vocab rename (crawls-captures-vocab-b1e) and faceted detail pages (facets-detail-pages-dgr) -- together these are the collection/crawl view polish pass. Ties to the Browsertrix importer epic (15z), which brings in exactly these screenshot-bearing WACZs.
close_reason: Delivered, with an og:image pivot: SUCHO/2022 crawls have no Browsertrix screenshots (zero urn: records), so the representative image is the crawl's main-page og:image (twitter:image fallback) instead. Pipeline: at index time (CDX-streamable WACZs), thumbnail::generate reads the main page's HTML, extracts og:image, range-fetches that captured image from the WACZ, downscales it (image crate, 400px), and caches <home>/index/thumbs/<crawl_id>.jpg. Served at GET /thumb/{id}; shown on collection cards + collection/crawl detail. No image => CSS-only placeholder (hash-tinted gradient, per the maintainer's suggestion). Best-effort throughout; needs a reindex to populate. Screenshot-source + per-collection curated images remain follow-ups.

title: Vocabulary: Crawls / Captures vs WACZ in the UI
description:
Question (maintainer): do users care more about "Crawls" or "Captures" than about a "WACZ"?

WACZ is a packaging format -- a technical container. Users of a web archive more likely think in terms of:
- Crawls: a crawl session (e.g. a Browsertrix crawl) -- the user-meaningful activity that produced the content. Often 1:1 with a WACZ today, but not necessarily (a WACZ could bundle multiple crawls; a crawl could span WACZs).
- Captures / mementos: individual snapshots of a URL at a point in time -- what users navigate temporally (repeat captures of the same URL over time; already grouped in results, per the Costa & Silva navigational/temporal finding cited in DESIGN).

This is an information-architecture / vocabulary decision, not (yet) code: audit where the UI foregrounds "WACZ" and decide whether "Crawl" and/or "Capture" would be clearer -- e.g. presenting a WACZ as a "crawl" in provenance/collection contexts while keeping "WACZ" only where the file/format is actually meant (downloads, fixity, replay source). Ties to the two-level collection model and the provenance framing (Maemura et al.).

Worth deciding the mental model rustyweb presents before layering more UI (facets on detail pages, cross-WACZ discovery) on top of it, since those features inherit whatever vocabulary we choose. Discussion/decision issue; the outcome may spawn concrete rename/relabel tasks.
close_reason: Relabeled the UI to lead with 'Crawl': /wacz/{id} -> /crawl/{id} (route + handler + view), collection 'Crawls' count/heading, 'N crawls' on cards, crawl-detail empty states. Kept WACZ for file/format contexts (index/reindex CLI, /files byte-range, replay source, fixity) and the data model (Wacz/waczs.json). Documented the vocab split in DESIGN. 'Captures'/mementos vocab remains a separate later concern (out of scope).

comments.author: edsu
comments.text:
Decision (maintainer, after using the app): lead with "Crawl" as the primary user-facing noun. Most users don't care about WACZ (a packaging format); they think in terms of crawls. So this moves from an open IA question to a concrete relabel/rename task.

Direction:
- Present a WACZ as a "Crawl" in user-facing surfaces: the /wacz/{id} detail page becomes the crawl view; collection pages list "Crawls" (not "WACZs"); homepage cards say "N crawls" (not "N WACZs").
- Keep "WACZ" ONLY where the file/format is genuinely meant: the crawl detail "File" section (Source / Size / SHA-256 / download), the replay source, fixity/integrity, and the `rustyweb index archive/*.wacz` CLI docs.
- Route: consider renaming /wacz/{id} -> /crawl/{id} (early, few users, cleaner long-term) with the old path optionally 301-redirecting; or keep /wacz/{id} internally and only change labels. Decide as part of the work.

Nuance to keep in mind (don't over-engineer now): in Browsertrix an item can be a "Crawl" OR an "Upload"; a crawl could in principle span multiple WACZs and a WACZ could bundle multiple crawls. Today it's ~1:1, so "Crawl" is a safe default label. Later we can refine from Browsertrix item metadata (15z.6) to say "Crawl" vs "Upload" precisely.

"Captures" / mementos (individual URL snapshots over time) remain a SEPARATE, later concern -- those are the temporally-grouped snapshots in search results, not this rename. Out of scope here.

Concrete surfaces to change (audited in views.rs):
- home card: "(c.count) WACZ[s]" -> "crawl[s]" (views.rs:162)
- home empty state text mentions archive/*.wacz (keep -- that's the CLI/format) (views.rs:153)
- collection page: h2 "WACZs" -> "Crawls"; "No WACZs in this collection." -> "No crawls ..." (views.rs:440-442)
- crawl (WACZ) detail: page framing/headings; "No pages are listed in this WACZ." -> "... in this crawl." (views.rs:520); keep the "File" section as WACZ/format.
- MemberItem / WaczPage struct + fn names can stay as-is internally (WACZ is correct at the data layer); this is a presentation-layer relabel.

Bumping to P2 and starting now (ties to the blog post / screenshots the maintainer is preparing). Relates to facets-on-detail (dgr) and representative-image (new) -- the three together are the collection/crawl view polish pass.

title: Cross-WACZ link resolution during replay
description:
Problem: replay is WACZ-direct and scoped to ONE WACZ. The viewer loads a single source (viewer_source(col) -> /replay/viewer?source=<wacz>&url=&ts=), so wabac.js resolves links only within that WACZ. If a replayed page links to a URL that isn't in the loaded WACZ but IS archived in ANOTHER WACZ in the collection/archive, the link fails to replay -- even though rustyweb has that URL indexed elsewhere. This undercuts the "reading room" goal of navigating the archive as a whole.

What we already have to build on:
- Tantivy index maps url -> collection/WACZ (+ timestamp) for indexed PAGES (fast, exact-match lookup on the url field).
- search-url opens each WACZ's internal CDX and returns records matching a URL, so it can resolve ANY archived URL (not just pages) to the WACZ(s) that hold it -- at the cost of reading CDXs.

Candidate approaches (to evaluate):
1. Index-driven redirect (lightweight): add a resolve API (e.g. GET /api/resolve?url= -> [{wacz_id, ts}]) backed by the index for pages. When a replayed page navigates to a URL the current WACZ lacks, consult the API and re-launch the viewer against the WACZ that has it (a "this link lives in another crawl -- open it" jump). Low browser footprint; needs a way to detect the miss / intercept navigation inside ReplayWeb.page.
2. Multi-WACZ collection replay (native): load a ReplayWeb.page multi-WACZ collection (a JSON manifest listing the collection's member WACZ sources) instead of a single WACZ, so wabac.js merges their indexes and resolves any URL present in the collection natively. Cleanest UX; but the browser loads each member WACZ's index -- a scale concern for large/institutional collections. VERIFY ReplayWeb.page multi-WACZ support + how it scopes/loads indexes.
3. Hybrid / lazy: start with the page's own WACZ; on a miss, resolve via the index and dynamically add the target WACZ to the wabac collection, so only WACZs actually needed get loaded.

Open questions:
- How to detect a cross-WACZ miss / hook link navigation inside the ReplayWeb.page iframe (does it expose events / postMessage, or do we intercept clicks pre-replay?).
- Page-level resolution (the click-a-link case) vs full resource-level (sub-resources like images/CSS from another WACZ) -- the latter is deeper and likely out of scope for v1.
- Scale: avoid loading an entire large collection's indexes into the browser (ties to rustyweb-scale-footprint-qw5).
- Which URL/timestamp to pick when multiple WACZs/captures have the target (closest in time? most recent? same collection first?).

Scope note: v1 could target page-to-page links within a single curated collection, resolved via the index, as the highest-value slice. Relates to the two-level collection model, search-url, and the outbound-links link-graph task (rustyweb-outbound-links-ind).

comments.author: edsu
comments.text:
Design caution (temporal coherence): relevant prior work from Michael Nelson's team at ODU (the WS-DL group) on temporal violations in composite mementos -- a replayed page can stitch together embedded resources captured at different times, yielding a composite that never actually existed as presented. See Ainsworth, Nelson & Van de Sompel, "Only One Out of Five Archived Web Pages Existed as Presented" (ACM Hypertext 2015), and the broader WS-DL work on temporal coherence / prima facie violations (the Internet Archive Wayback Machine transcluding older captures of embedded resources into a page).

Why it matters here: cross-WACZ resolution deliberately pulls a URL from a DIFFERENT WACZ -- i.e. a different crawl at a (possibly very) different time. Doing so can INTRODUCE exactly these temporal violations, presenting a page that never existed. So the "which WACZ/timestamp to pick" open question above is not just a convenience choice -- it is a correctness concern:
- Prefer captures close to the root page's memento-datetime; bound and/or surface the temporal spread to the user rather than silently substituting a far-off capture.
- Page-to-page link navigation (following a link to another archived page) is lower risk than transcluding a sub-resource (image/CSS/JS) from another WACZ into the current page -- the latter is the classic temporal-violation case and should be treated with extra caution (or left out of v1).
- Consider making cross-WACZ substitution visible/optional (e.g. "this link is archived in another crawl from <date> -- open it?") rather than seamless, so the user knows they've crossed a temporal boundary.

comments.author: edsu
comments.text:
Design reframing (maintainer): single-WACZ replay scope is a FEATURE, not a bug. Because replay is scoped to one WACZ, a rendered page only pulls resources captured in that same crawl -- which is precisely what preserves temporal coherence and avoids the ODU-documented temporal violations (a page stitched from resources of different times that never existed as presented). Pulling a JS file or image from another time period into the page would be the bug; NOT doing so is the virtue.

Consequences for this feature:
- Do NOT merge WACZs for replay or transclude sub-resources (JS/CSS/images) across WACZs -- that would recreate the temporal-violation problem. This deprioritizes the "native multi-WACZ merged replay" approach and rules out cross-WACZ sub-resource resolution.
- Reframe the cross-WACZ case around MISSING PAGES (link targets), not resources: when a link's target isn't in the current WACZ, use the index to surface where else in the archive it was captured -- list the other WACZs/crawls that hold it, WITH their capture dates and provenance -- as an explicit discovery affordance. Opening one starts a fresh, temporally-coherent single-WACZ replay in THAT crawl's context.
- Make the temporal/provenance boundary explicit: the goal is to let the user consciously choose to look at another capture, understanding the crawl context is changing -- not to silently substitute content into the current page.

Net: this is primarily a DISCOVERY feature (fits the reading-room ethos: "this link isn't in this crawl; here's where else in the archive to find it, and when it was captured"), layered on index lookups -- not a replay-fidelity change. Single-WACZ replay stays coherent and untouched.

Documentation: when this is implemented, note the connection to web-archives research in README.md and DESIGN.md -- both temporal coherence / temporal violations (ODU WS-DL; Ainsworth, Nelson & Van de Sompel, HT 2015) and the framing of single-WACZ scope as a coherence feature. DESIGN.md already grounds the discovery features in the literature (Costa & Silva; Maemura et al.; the SHINE / SolrWayback lineage), so this fits alongside them.

title: Faceted overview on collection (and WACZ) detail pages
description:
Idea (maintainer): show facets on the collection detail page (/collection/{id}), scoped to that collection -- and evaluate the same for the individual WACZ detail page (/wacz/{id}).

Today: the homepage shows an archive-wide facet overview (facet_overview()) and the search page has the full facet sidebar (collection, site, date, type, language). The collection page shows metadata + member WACZs; the WACZ page shows provenance + seed pages -- neither shows facets.

Proposal: compute a SCOPED facet overview on these detail pages, reusing the existing faceting (search_faceted supports filters; facet fields include site, year/month timeline, type, lang, and status). Scope by FIELD_COLLECTION (the curated collection) on the collection page, and FIELD_COLLECTION_ID (the per-WACZ id) on the WACZ page. Render the familiar breakdown (top sites, a month timeline, types, languages) with each value linking into a scoped search. This turns each collection into a faceted "reading room" entry point rather than just a member list.

Individual WACZ: same idea scoped to one WACZ. The collection facet is moot there, but site/date/type/language/status still help convey what a crawl captured. Evaluate value vs clutter (a single WACZ may be small) -- likely lower priority than the collection page.

Building blocks already exist (facet_overview / search_faceted + the facet sidebar view); this is mostly wiring a scoped facet query into the two detail handlers and reusing the sidebar rendering. Relates to the faceted-discovery work.
close_reason: Both detail pages now carry the scoped facet overview. Crawl page done via FacetScope::Crawl + collection_id as a recognized search filter (chip resolves the opaque WACZ id to the crawl's name). Collection page shipped earlier in the stack.

title: Index extracted page text from Browsertrix pages.jsonl / extraPages.jsonl (fixes JS-rendered content being unsearchable)
description:
Index the extracted page `text` from Browsertrix pages.jsonl / extraPages.jsonl.

Root cause found while debugging a SUCHO search miss: searching the homepage term "Петиція" (on https://uinp.gov.ua/) returns nothing, even though it's clearly visible in replay. The term is JS-rendered, so it is NOT in the raw HTML response body (confirmed). Browsertrix captured the rendered text, but for this 2022-vintage crawl it wrote it into the WACZ's pages files, NOT as `urn:text:` WARC records:
- CDX has 30044 records and ZERO `urn:` records (no urn:text:).
- pages/pages.jsonl (seed pages) and pages/extraPages.jsonl (4150 pages here) each have a `text` field per entry.
- The homepage pages.jsonl entry has text (2708 chars) that DOES contain "Петиція".

rustyweb currently reads pages.jsonl only for seed-page metadata (url/title/ts -> seed_pages) and ignores the `text` field; it never reads extraPages.jsonl. Body text is taken from urn:text: (absent here) or scraped HTML (missing JS-rendered content). Result: rendered content is unsearchable and recall is badly degraded for Browsertrix crawls of this shape -- including the flagship SUCHO demo.

Fix: during indexing, also read the `text` (and title) from pages/pages.jsonl AND pages/extraPages.jsonl, and merge it into the per-URL page body -- treat it like Browsertrix rendered text (same role as the urn:text: path). Prefer rendered text (urn:text OR pages-jsonl text) over scraped HTML for the body; keep HTML title/description/lang where richer.

Considerations:
- Both index paths must do it: the default CDX-guided/streaming path (collect_page_records_via_cdx) does not currently read pages.jsonl at all for text; the full-scan path (index_wacz) doesn't either. The pages files are small zip entries -- cheap to read once during setup (the streaming setup already opens the ZIP central directory).
- Merge by URL into the existing MergedPage (add a source, e.g. jsonl_text, slotted with rendered_text). Decide precedence: urn:text: vs pages.jsonl text (they're the same kind of data; either is fine -- prefer whichever is present).
- Skip the header line ({format,id,title}) in each jsonl. Handle both pages.jsonl and extraPages.jsonl; tolerate absence (py-wacz and some WACZs have no text).
- Pages that exist only in extraPages.jsonl (not surfaced as a page today) should become searchable pages, tagged to the WACZ/collection like any other page. Watch doc counts (this could add many docs -- here ~4150/WACZ).
- Titles from pages.jsonl may be better than scraped ones; consider using them as a fallback.
- Timestamps: pages.jsonl has ts; use for the page if the HTML capture ts is missing (mirror the urn:text merge which only fills an empty ts).
- Add a regression test with a fixture WACZ that has pages.jsonl text but the term absent from HTML, asserting the term is searchable after indexing.

Impact: HIGH -- fixes a large class of "I can see it in replay but can't search it" misses on the SUCHO demo and directly improves the Browsertrix importer (15z) results, since those are exactly these WACZs. P1.

Relates to the Browsertrix importer epic (rustyweb-15z) and the urn:text: rendered-text handling already in index.rs (record_to_raw / index_merged).
close_reason: Implemented: read the text field from pages/pages.jsonl + pages/extraPages.jsonl and fold it into the per-URL page body (interchangeable with urn:text:), in both the CDX-guided and full-scan paths. Unit test (read_page_texts) + end-to-end test (term present only in pages.jsonl is searchable via the default path). Confirmed root cause on the SUCHO WACZ: CDX had zero urn:text records; the rendered text (incl. the missing term) lives in pages.jsonl.

title: Production deployment in the cloud (reverse-proxy fronted)
description:
Set up a production deployment of rustyweb in the cloud, fronted by a modern reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy, or similar) for TLS and public-facing concerns. rustyweb is a single self-contained binary that serves HTTP via axum (default 127.0.0.1:8080); the deployment wraps that for real-world hosting while keeping the 'range' ethos: the same setup should suit a small VPS and scale up toward an institutional host.

Goal: a documented, reproducible way to run rustyweb publicly (TLS, a domain, restart-on-failure) with the reverse proxy handling the edge and rustyweb serving search + WACZ bytes.

rustyweb-specific considerations:
- BYTE-RANGE PASSTHROUGH is critical: replay is WACZ-direct — the browser reads GET /files/{id} with HTTP Range requests via wabac.js. The proxy must pass Range/Accept-Ranges/Content-Range through untouched and not buffer whole (multi-GB) WACZ responses (e.g. nginx: proxy_buffering off / proxy_max_temp_file_size 0, or use a proxy that streams ranges well). Verify large-file range serving end to end through the proxy.
- TLS termination at the proxy (Let's Encrypt). Caddy does this automatically; nginx needs certbot. Pick one.
- Run rustyweb bound to localhost, as a non-root user, index opened read-only (serve already opens read-only); only the proxy is public.
- Persistent storage for <home> (archive/ + index/). Streaming remote WACZs keeps disk small (index only); a local archive needs a durable volume. Decide the storage model per target.
- Compression is already done in-process (tower-http); avoid double-compressing at the proxy. Don't gzip byte-range responses.
- CORS: same-origin replay (WACZ served by rustyweb) needs none; replaying a *remote* WACZ needs that remote host's CORS — document the distinction.
- Health/readiness endpoint for the proxy / orchestrator (likely need to add a lightweight /health route — none today).
- Observability: tracing logs to stdout/journald; access logs at the proxy.

Deployment shapes to evaluate (pick 1-2):
- Container: single static binary -> tiny distroless/scratch OCI image; run on a VM, Fly.io, Cloud Run, ECS, etc. Assets are embedded at build time, so the image is self-contained.
- VM + systemd: a systemd unit for rustyweb + the proxy config on a VPS/EC2/droplet.

Open decisions: which reverse proxy (Caddy = simplest TLS; nginx = ubiquitous), which cloud target (VM vs managed container platform), container vs systemd, and where <home> lives (ephemeral + stream-only, or a persistent volume).

Deliverables (candidate child tasks): reverse-proxy config with verified range passthrough (nginx + Caddy examples); Dockerfile / OCI image (distroless); systemd unit + VM deploy guide; /health endpoint; a Deployment section in README/DESIGN. Relates to the scale-footprint direction (rustyweb-scale-footprint-qw5) and to streaming (remote-only deploys need little disk).

comments.author: edsu

That makes prod-deploy the keystone -- the same packaging serves the hosted demo and the self-host recipe.

Direction: favor a docker-compose (rustyweb + Caddy for automatic HTTPS) as the one-command "bring up your own instance" story, reusing the existing Dockerfile; the same compose hosts the SUCHO demo. Also document the lightest-weight local path (cargo install + quickstart).

title: Make reindex resilient to transient remote-fetch failures
description:
`rustyweb reindex` currently fails fast: it drops the old full-text index up front, then re-indexes every registered source, and the first error (e.g. a transient network read on a remote WACZ) aborts the whole run — leaving the index partially rebuilt. With a collection of remote WACZs (e.g. the `sul` Stanford stacks collection) a single flaky fetch means the entire reindex is lost and must be restarted from scratch (re-fetching everything, ~20+ min), where it may fail again.

This bit us during the faceting work: reindex died at 9/19 on a transient read of a remote WACZ, and the Browsertrix collections (later in the manifest) never got indexed.

Desired behavior: a transient remote-fetch failure should be treated like a missing local file already is — logged as a warning and skipped, preserving the WACZ's manifest entry — so the rest of the reindex completes. At the end, print a summary of any skipped sources and exit non-zero (so cron/CI notices) while still leaving a usable, mostly-complete index.

Scope / considerations:
- Distinguish transient/remote-fetch errors (skip + warn) from programmer/logic errors (still fail). Start by making remote fetch/parse failures non-fatal; keep local validation (path outside archive, non-.wacz) fail-fast as today.
- Consider ret/retries with backoff for remote fetches before giving up on one.
- Consider not dropping the old index until the rebuild succeeds, or building into a temp dir and swapping — so a failed reindex doesn't destroy a working index. (Bigger change; could be a follow-up.)
- Report a clear end-of-run summary: N indexed, M skipped (with reasons).
- Tests: a source that errors is skipped and the run completes over the rest.

Relates to the remote-WACZ collections use case and the Browsertrix importer epic (rustyweb-15z), which will add more remote sources.
close_reason: Both remaining follow-ups delivered: reindex skip-on-failure (with non-zero exit + summary) and per-host concurrency cap (MAX_CONCURRENCY=64).

comments.author: edsu
comments.text:
Scope (maintainer concern): broaden this beyond "retry transient failures" to robust AND POLITE remote fetching. Now that CDX-guided record fetch is concurrent (r0n.7, default --concurrency 16), a single WACZ's requests all hit ONE host, so we can overload a small/self-hosted server or get IP-blocked when the target isn't a big object store like S3.

Key measure: honor HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) and 503 + the Retry-After header with exponential backoff, for both the range fetches (http_range::HttpFetch) and the whole-file downloads. This is simultaneously resilience (recover from transient/overload errors, per this task's original intent) AND politeness (back off when a host pushes back, instead of hammering -> far less likely to get blocked).

Also consider:
- A per-host rate and/or concurrency cap (proactive politeness), separate from the raw --concurrency worker count.
- Revisiting the default --concurrency: 16 is tuned for object stores; a lower default (or a "be gentle" mode) may be safer when pointing rustyweb at arbitrary hosts, which matters for the self-serve / adoption story.
- Retry with jittered backoff on transient network errors, and a bounded retry budget, so a long ingest survives blips without giving up or busy-looping.

Ties to the streaming epic (r0n) and the shareable/self-serve goal (others will point rustyweb at arbitrary hosts, not just S3).

comments.author: edsu
comments.text:
Core delivered in PR #36 (resilient + polite remote fetching):
- Retry transient failures (network errors + HTTP 429/502/503/504) with capped exponential backoff + jitter, honoring server Retry-After — applied to the range GETs (HttpFetch::open/fetch) and the whole-file downloads (get_reader).
- Agent built with http_status_as_error(false) so 4xx/5xx are inspectable (status + Retry-After).
- Gentle default --concurrency (4 remote / CPU count local) so we don't overload/get-blocked by default.
- Tests: with_retry unit tests + an axum 503-then-200 integration test (get_reader_retries_a_transient_status).

Remaining follow-ups (keep this task open for them):
- reindex skip-on-failure: don't abort the whole `reindex` run when one source fails after retries — skip it with a warning (like missing local files) so a long rebuild survives one bad/blocked source.
- Per-host rate/concurrency cap (proactive politeness beyond the raw worker count).

comments.author: edsu
comments.text:
Remaining follow-ups delivered (this closes the task):

1. reindex skip-on-failure — `reindex` no longer aborts the whole rebuild when one source fails. A source that can't be indexed (missing local file, or a remote source still failing after the retry budget) is skipped with a per-source warning, and its manifest entry is preserved. The rebuild always runs to completion and the mostly-rebuilt index is committed (usable). Per the desired behavior here, if anything was skipped the command exits non-zero with a summary count (indexed N of M, skipped K) so cron/CI notices while still leaving a usable index. Test: reindex_skips_a_failing_source_and_keeps_going (good + corrupt source → completes over the good one, returns non-zero, both manifest entries preserved).

2. Per-host concurrency cap — the resolved fetch worker count is clamped to a per-host ceiling (MAX_CONCURRENCY = 64), so no --concurrency value (or high local core count) can put an unbounded number of range requests in flight against a single host. A mis-typed --concurrency 500 logs a warning and is capped. Proactive politeness on top of the existing retry/backoff + gentle default (4 remote).

Docs updated (README + DESIGN). Not doing the bigger "build into a temp dir and atomic-swap so a failed reindex never touches the working index" idea from the original scope — the resilience + non-zero-exit here covers the immediate need; can be a separate follow-up if wanted.
