title: Scale: from a laptop to institutional TBs
description:
Keep rustyweb's index frugal, predictable, and fast as a corpus grows from a laptop's handful of WACZs to an institution's TBs of WARC — without degrading the small/local experience. rustyweb's identity is small→institutional (unlike SolrWayback/SHINE, which assume a Solr cluster), so scale has to be a first-class concern of the single-binary design.

Measured baseline (2026-07, ~21.7k docs / 118 MB index): stored text (.store) is ~64% of the index and scales linearly with the corpus; term positions (.pos) ~20%; the inverted index (.term/.idx) ~16%; fast fields / facets ~0%. So the dominant, corpus-linear cost is the text we store for snippets — that is the thing to attack at scale.

The levers interact: capping stored body text shrinks .store AND makes the per-query URL-grouping pass (which reads up to CANDIDATE_CAP stored docs) cheaper. Frugality should be configurable: a laptop keeps generous snippets, an institution dials it down.

Scope covers on-disk footprint, query-time performance/memory, index build at scale, and the one-index-vs-sharding architectural question. Every decision should be driven by the size/scale model (first task), not intuition — several child tasks are investigations that may resolve to "leave as is".

title: Size and scale model (measure-first)
description: Measure-first foundation that gates the rest of the epic. Build a repeatable size/scale model: bytes-per-doc broken down by Tantivy file type (.store, .pos, .term, .idx, .fast, .fieldnorm), projected to 1M / 100M docs and to TB-scale corpora; plus index build time and peak RAM for a known corpus. Output a short written model (checked into the repo or DESIGN.md) and a script/command to re-measure after changes. Goal: choose every other lever from data, and know how alarmed to actually be. Baseline today: ~21.7k docs / 118 MB, .store ~64%.

title: Docstore compression audit and tuning
description: The document store (.store) is ~64% of the index and holds the stored text used for snippets. Find out which compressor Tantivy uses for the docstore today (default vs configured) and benchmark alternatives (zstd, brotli) for size vs CPU on a representative corpus. Potentially a large, zero-behavior-change win before we cap anything. Deliver: current setting documented, a size/CPU comparison, and a recommendation (and switch, if clearly better). Investigation that may resolve to "default is fine".

title: Cap stored body text with snippet fallback
description: Headline size lever. Cap how much body text is *stored* (e.g. first ~8-16 KB), independent of what is *indexed* (the full body stays indexed, so recall is unaffected — only snippet display for deep-only matches degrades). Extend the results snippet fallback chain: highlighted body snippet -> page description -> a plain leading excerpt of the stored prefix -> title/URL only. Optionally show a subtle "match deeper in page" note when the hit is past the cap. Cap size should be configurable (see the configurable-frugality task). UTF-8-safe truncation. Requires reindex.

title: Positions audit: drop where phrase search is not needed
description: Term positions (.pos) are ~20% of the index and exist to support phrase queries. Audit which TEXT fields actually need phrase search: keep positions on body (and probably title), consider dropping them on description and headings (set_index_option to a positionless option). Small, corpus-linear win. Requires reindex. Confirm phrase queries on the kept fields still work and the dropped ones still match as bag-of-words.

title: Query-time cost of faceting/grouping at scale
description: Benchmark query-time cost at scale on a large synthetic index: facet terms aggregations and the URL-grouping pass in search_faceted (which currently reads up to CANDIDATE_CAP=1000 *stored* docs per query just to read each candidate's URL). Concrete synergy with the size work: make `url` a FAST field so grouping reads a fast column instead of stored docs — cuts per-query latency and pairs with the stored-body cap (fewer/smaller stored reads). Also revisit whether CANDIDATE_CAP should scale with corpus. Requires reindex if url becomes fast.

title: Index build at scale (RAM, merges, resumable ingest)
description: Make indexing large corpora practical: size the IndexWriter memory buffer (currently 50 MB) and document/parallelize ingest under a RAM ceiling, review the segment merge policy at high doc counts, and make large ingests resumable/incremental (skip already-indexed sources; recover from a partial run) — related to the reindex-resilience task. Deliver guidance + any config knobs for ingesting TBs without OOM or multi-day runs.

title: Sharding investigation (one index vs per-collection)
description: Research spike (not a build): at billions of docs / TBs, does one Tantivy index remain workable, or does rustyweb need to shard — e.g. one index per collection, or by time/size — with query-time fan-in? Weigh single-index simplicity against per-shard build/merge/memory limits and Tantivy's practical ceilings. Decide IF and WHEN sharding is needed and what the trigger metric is (doc count / index size / query latency from the scale model). Likely outcome: "not yet, revisit at threshold X" — documented so it is a conscious decision, not a surprise.

title: Configurable frugality (laptop vs institution presets)
description: Make frugality a first-class, configurable choice rather than hardcoded, so the same binary serves both ends of the range: a laptop keeps generous snippets while an institution dials size down. Surface the knobs from the other tasks — stored-body cap size, docstore compressor, (maybe) which fields keep positions — via config (file and/or flags), with a laptop-generous default and an institution "frugal" profile/preset. Depends on the cap and compression tasks landing first.

title: Search enrichment inspired by SolrWayback / warc-indexer
description:
Index more per-record and HTML signals as queryable fields to improve archive search, inspired by SolrWayback and the British Library warc-indexer (which runs Tika/DROID and derives a large Solr schema). rustyweb adopts only lightweight, pure-Rust pieces that fit the single-binary, no-heavy-deps philosophy; full-text search over HTML/PDF/rendered-text stays the core and is unaffected.

Each new field is a schema addition, so it requires a rebuild via rustyweb reindex (the schema guard already errors and points there). Extraction lives in extract_html_text / collect_page_records; schema in build_schema; query wiring in SearchIndex::search.

In scope (child tasks):
- meta keywords + author (HTML metadata)
- content-based language detection fallback (whatlang)
- registrable-domain (public suffix) for a site: filter spanning subdomains
- outbound link host/domain fields (link-based discovery / future link graph)
- last-modified date facet

Deferred / out of scope for now (keep it minimal): image enrichment (dimensions, EXIF geo, perceptual/PDQ image search), entity/geo extraction (locations, postcodes), fuzzy near-duplicate hashing (ssdeep), elements-used analytics, and Tika-style text extraction from arbitrary binary formats (needs a heavy extractor). Open question also deferred: whether to index non-text resources (images/js/css) as stub docs to power a real media-type facet.

Refs: https://github.com/netarchivesuite/solrwayback and warc-indexer SolrFields.java in https://github.com/ukwa/webarchive-discovery
notes: Its new fields (registrable-domain site:, keywords/author, outbound links) feed the facets in rustyweb-faceted-discovery-4js (faceted discovery epic).
close_reason: done

title: Index HTML meta keywords and author
description: Extract meta keywords (meta[name=keywords]) and author (meta[name=author], with og/article fallbacks) during HTML extraction and index them. Add keywords (TEXT, searchable via the default query fields) and author (TEXT, searchable and usable as author:name) to the schema; populate via HtmlText and the Page struct, mirroring the existing description/headings work. Mirrors warc-indexer keywords/author. Low effort. Requires reindex.
close_reason: done

title: Content-based language detection fallback
description: When a page has no html lang attribute, detect the language from the extracted body text with a lightweight pure-Rust detector (whatlang) and store its primary subtag in the existing lang field. Keep the html lang attribute as the primary source; only fall back when it is absent or empty. Improves coverage of the lang: filter. Mirrors warc-indexer content_language (which uses Tika). Adds the whatlang dependency (small). Requires reindex.
close_reason: done

title: Registrable-domain field for a cross-subdomain site: filter
description: Add a registrable-domain field (the private domain: one label above the public suffix, via the public suffix list / psl crate) so a site: filter matches a whole site across subdomains and handles multi-level suffixes like example.co.uk. Today domain holds the raw host (www.example.com), so subdomains and the bare domain do not unify. Keep domain (host) for exact host filtering and add site (registrable domain) for cross-subdomain filtering; both derived from the page URL. Mirrors the warc-indexer host / domain / public_suffix split. Adds the psl dependency (bundled public suffix list) - confirm it fits the single-binary goal. Highest search-quality win for archives with many subdomains. Requires reindex.
close_reason: done

title: Index outbound link hosts and domains
description: Extract outbound hyperlink targets (a[href], resolved against the page URL) and index their hosts and domains as multi-valued fields (links_hosts, links_domains), enabling queries like links_hosts:youtube.com (pages that link to a host) and a future link-graph view. Mirrors warc-indexer links_hosts / links_domains / links_images. Extraction is easy with scraper; note the index-size cost of storing link targets. Decide whether to also capture links_images. Requires reindex.
close_reason: Moved to standalone task rustyweb-outbound-links-ind (deferred)

title: Last-Modified date facet
description: Capture the HTTP Last-Modified response header (response records already parse http_headers) as a second date signal: store last_modified and/or last_modified_year for filtering, complementing crawl year. Mirrors warc-indexer last_modified / last_modified_year. Small; helps distinguish when content was authored vs when it was crawled. Requires reindex.
close_reason: done

title: Index HTTP status_code for capture-level status filtering
description: Index the HTTP status_code (and optionally expose per-capture status/mime/digest) so results can be filtered, e.g. status:200 vs redirects/errors. Moved from the provenance epic (was rustyweb-provenance-imd.6); it is a search/filter feature rather than provenance.
close_reason: done

title: Optional golden search integration test (full-featured WACZ)
description:
Add an OPTIONAL, opt-in integration test that indexes a known full-featured WACZ and asserts search results look correct end to end -- a "golden" search test.

Motivation: the committed fixtures (a.wacz, simple.wacz, pdf-doc.wacz) are small, so they don't exercise search the way real content does -- diverse sites/dates/languages/types for faceting, phrase search over real prose, ranking across many docs, PDFs + Browsertrix rendered urn:text together. This would catch regressions that small fixtures miss (e.g. the phrase-search behavior the maintainer found confusing) and guard the search feature as indexing internals change (parallel fetch, setup consolidation, etc.).

Optional / opt-in because a full-featured WACZ is large and not a good committed fixture in the default test run:
- Gate with #[ignore] (run via `cargo test -- --ignored`) and/or an env var pointing at the WACZ (e.g. RUSTYWEB_SEARCH_WACZ=path-or-url), skipping cleanly when unset so normal CI stays fast.
- Source options to decide: a curated medium fixture committed via git-lfs; a stable public WACZ URL (streamed); or a local path supplied by the developer.

Assertions to cover (the "looks correct" part):
- Representative queries return the expected top page(s) with the right fields (title/url/collection).
- Phrase search: a quoted phrase matches the page containing that exact adjacent phrase and excludes near-misses (the per-field, OR-across-fields semantics).
- Filters: site:, domain:, type:, lang:, status:, year: (and ranges) narrow as expected.
- Facet counts for the main dimensions match known values for the corpus.
- Ranking sanity (title match outranks body match), snippets present, PDF text and rendered urn:text both searchable.

Relates to the search feature generally and the phrase-search question raised during the r0n.7 work.

title: Searchable page metadata: domain, URL tokens, title boost, AND-by-default + in-app search help
description:
Improve full-text search relevance and expressiveness, and document the query syntax in the app.

Current state: QueryParser has default fields [title, body] only; url/timestamp are STRING (exact-match only) so domain/URL search does not usefully work; multi-word queries are OR by default; no in-app help.

Scope (this slice):
- Schema: add 'domain' (STRING|STORED, exact host for domain: filter + display) and 'url_tokens' (TEXT, tokenized URL words) to the Tantivy schema.
- Extraction: derive host + tokenized URL text from each page URL (url crate).
- Query: default fields become [title, body, url_tokens]; set_conjunction_by_default() (AND); boost title over body; use lenient parsing so malformed queries do not 500.
- Docs: in-app 'Search tips' disclosure on homepage + results page documenting the real working syntax (phrases, field search title:/domain:, +/-, OR, grouping, boost).
- Update DESIGN.md schema table + README search note.
- Tests for domain extraction, url tokens, domain: filtering, AND semantics.

Note: adding schema fields requires re-indexing existing collections (idempotent upsert).

Deferred follow-ups: crawl-date as DATE/numeric range field, meta_description/OpenGraph, headings, lang/mime filters.
close_reason: Implemented: domain + url_tokens fields, AND-by-default, title boost, lenient parsing, in-app Search tips, DESIGN/README updates. Tests green.

title: Show HTTP status on search results; evaluate faceting by status
description:
Idea (maintainer): surface HTTP response status on the search results page, and assess whether faceting by status is worthwhile.

Current state (already partly there):
- HTTP status IS indexed: FIELD_STATUS ("status"), INDEXED | STORED, populated from page.status (WARC http_status).
- It IS queryable: status:200 and range syntax status:[200 TO 299] (shown in the search tips).
- It IS an EXTRA_FILTER (a filterable field, labelled "Status") -- so it can be filtered, but it is NOT one of the primary sidebar FACET_DIMENSIONS (collection, site, date, type, language), and it is NOT displayed on individual result cards.

Part 1 -- show status on results (cheap, worthwhile): add the status code to each result card. Most indexed pages are 200, so the real value is flagging the exceptions (archived 404 / 500 / error pages, or non-200 captures), e.g. a small badge shown prominently only for non-200 and subtle/omitted for 200.

Part 2 -- is faceting worthwhile? (measure first): page documents are overwhelmingly 200 -- we index HTML/PDF response records that became pages; redirects and no-body records generally aren't page docs -- so a full status facet likely has low diversity and limited value for a scarce sidebar slot. Options:
- Leave as-is (queryable via status: + the existing EXTRA_FILTER).
- Add a lightweight "non-200 only" filter/toggle -- probably the highest-value slice (find archived error pages).
- Promote status to FACET_DIMENSIONS only if a real index shows meaningful spread. Measure the actual status distribution before committing a facet.

Caveat: soft-404s (HTTP 200 with "not found" content) won't be caught by status alone -- status is not a content-correctness signal.

Relates to the faceted-discovery work and the EXTRA_FILTERS mechanism.

title: Streaming / CDX-guided indexing (index WACZs without full download)
description:
Index WACZ content selectively via the WACZ's internal CDX instead of downloading the whole WACZ and parsing every WARC record.

Today (index_one -> download_to_temp): a remote WACZ is fully downloaded to a temp file, opened as a ZIP, and every archive/*.warc.gz record is decompressed and inspected. The CDX index inside the WACZ (indexes/index.cdx.gz) already lists, per capture, the mime, WARC filename, offset, and length — and rustyweb already parses these (wacz.rs search_cdx / CdxjRecord). So we can identify just the html/text/pdf (and Browsertrix urn:text) records and seek to them: the CDX offset/length is into the named WARC, and that WARC is a stored (uncompressed) ZIP entry in the WACZ, so absolute range = zip_data_start(filename) + offset .. +length; the slice is one gzip member -> one WARC record.

For remote WACZs this is done with HTTP range requests, exactly like the wabac.js replay client. Make streaming vs full-download an OPT-IN choice; keep download-to-temp as the default/fallback.

Wins: avoid full remote download (bandwidth, transient disk / the TMPDIR problem) — big for media-heavy crawls where text is a small fraction of bytes; skip decompressing/parsing media records even locally (CPU). Costs: many small range requests are round-trip-latency-bound and can be SLOWER than one big GET for text-heavy remotes (coalescing adjacent records helps only when text clusters); more code (HTTP-range ZIP reading, per-record gzip seeking).

Two gotchas to decide up front: (1) whole-file SHA-256 fixity can't be computed without reading the whole file, so streamed sources need a different integrity model (per-record WARC-Payload-Digest from the CDX, or the datapackage-digest); (2) the cross-WARC per-URL merge (rendered urn:text often lives in a different WARC than the HTML) and PDF handling must be preserved by the CDX-guided selector.

Relates to the scale epic (rustyweb-scale-footprint-qw5) and reindex resilience (rustyweb-reindex-resilient-aev). Measure-first: benchmark before committing to defaults.
design:
Feasibility validated (2026-07) against a real Browsertrix WACZ:
- WARCs are Stored (uncompressed) inside the WACZ ZIP, so a CDX record's byte offset maps to an absolute WACZ position: abs = zip_data_start(entry) + cdx.offset, read cdx.length bytes = one gzip member = one WARC record. Proven end-to-end (offset -> gzip magic -> WARC/1.1 with matching WARC-Target-URI).
- The zip crate (2.4.2) exposes ZipFile::data_start() and ZipArchive<R: Read + Seek>, so it parses the central directory for us over ANY Read+Seek source.
- Gotcha: the CDX `filename` is the bare WARC name (rec-*.warc.gz); the ZIP entry is under archive/ — map by suffix.

Design: ONE CDX-guided extractor over a pluggable Read+Seek reader.
- Local source -> std::fs::File (Phase 1, .1).
- Remote source -> an HttpRangeReader (Read+Seek over ureq range GETs; total size from the first range's Content-Range; read-ahead buffer; coalesce adjacent wanted records) handed to ZipArchive (Phase 2, .2).
- Extractor: read all CDX records, select by mime (html/pdf + Browsertrix urn:text), seek to abs offset, gunzip the slice, parse the single WARC record, feed the existing merge/index pipeline. Reuses CDX parsing, WARC record parsing, HTML/PDF extraction, and the merge.

DEFAULT MODEL (decided with maintainer): a remote WACZ stays remote for both indexing and replay.
- Remote default = STREAM index (range) + replay from the URL (already the case). Nothing colocated.
- `--download` is the opt-in: colocate into <home>/archive (durable copy, faster bulk indexing, whole-file fixity, offline replay). Unifies with the Browsertrix always-download flow.
- Local WACZ unchanged.

Consequences: (1) fixity — streamed remotes have no whole-file SHA-256; v1 marks them "not locally verifiable" (verify reports that, not MODIFIED), per-record WARC-Payload-Digest is a later option; --download keeps full fixity. (2) robustness/perf — many small range requests can be slower and are exposed to flaky hosts; --download is the robust escape hatch. (3) streaming trusts the CDX to enumerate records (authoritative for Browsertrix).

comments.author: edsu
comments.text:
Follow-up optimization spotted while using r0n.7 (parallel record fetch): now that the per-record fetch is concurrent, the serial "reading index" SETUP phase dominates for multi-GB remote WACZs.

The setup iterates all ZIP entries ~4 times, each a serial by_index pass that reads scattered local headers over the network:
1. remote_warcs_streamable / local_warcs_streamable (the stream-vs-download pre-check in index_one)
2. ensure_warcs_stored (in collect_page_records_via_cdx)
3. warc_data_starts (in collect)
4. find_warcinfo_streaming's name collection (in collect)

Biggest, safest win: CONSOLIDATE these into a single by_index pass that gathers, per WARC entry, its compression (storedness), data_start, and name, plus the first WARC's warcinfo -- and fold the storedness decision so the separate *_warcs_streamable pre-check isn't a fourth full iteration. No zip-internals reimplementation; should cut the setup roughly 4x.

Further (optional, measure first): parallelize the remaining single pass by fetching local headers concurrently via RangeFetch and computing data_start from the local-header extra-field length. Feasible but reimplements part of the zip local-header parsing, so only if consolidation isn't enough. Also: read the CDX as one large range rather than CHUNK-by-CHUNK.

title: CDX-guided record extraction for local WACZs (Phase 1)
description: Phase 1 (local, no HTTP) — de-risks the parsing/seeking before any networking. Use the WACZ CDX (mime, filename, offset, length; already parsed by wacz.rs) to seek directly to the html/text/pdf and Browsertrix urn:text records in the local WARCs, instead of iterating and decompressing every record. Each CDX offset points into the named WARC; that WARC is a stored ZIP entry, so seek to zip_data_start(filename)+offset and read `length` bytes = one gzip member = one WARC record. Preserve the existing cross-WARC per-URL merge (rendered urn:text may live in a different WARC than the HTML) and PDF handling. Factor the per-record extractor so Phase 2 (HTTP range) can reuse it. Benchmark local index time vs the current scan-everything approach.
close_reason: CDX-guided extractor + --stream opt-in landed; parity (same page count as scan) + deflate-guard tested on fixtures

title: HTTP range reader for remote WACZs (Phase 2)
description: Phase 2 — a reader that fetches WACZ bytes over HTTP range requests (like wabac.js) so remote WACZs can be indexed without a full download. Needs: read the ZIP end-of-central-directory + central directory (small, at file end) to map each WARC filename to its stored data offset; then for each wanted CDX record, range-fetch zip_data_start(filename)+offset .. +length and gunzip the single member. Coalesce adjacent wanted records into fewer, larger range GETs to cut request count. Reuse the Phase 1 per-record extractor. Requires the host to support range requests (already required for replay). Reuse ureq (already used for downloads and range serving).
close_reason: HttpRangeReader landed; verified streaming a real 323MB remote WACZ over range requests (8 pages, same as download+scan, ~3s, no download)

title: Opt-in stream vs download mode for index
description: Let the user choose streaming vs full download per index run, e.g. index --stream / --download (default download-to-temp, which stays the fallback). Consider auto-selecting stream for remote sources once it is proven. Wire the choice into index_one so it picks the CDX-guided range reader (Phase 2) or the current download path. Keep behavior identical for local files unless streaming is requested. Document that streaming trades bandwidth/disk for more requests (can be slower for text-heavy remotes).
notes: Reframed: remote WACZs STREAM by default; --download is the opt-in to colocate into archive (durable, faster bulk, whole-file fixity, offline replay). Local unchanged.
close_reason: remote streams by default; --download colocates into archive; range/deflate fallback to download. Verified on a 323MB remote WACZ.

title: Fixity/integrity model for streamed (not fully downloaded) WACZs
description: Whole-file SHA-256 fixity (stored in the manifest, checked by `verify`) can't be computed when we never read the whole WACZ. Decide and implement the integrity model for streamed sources: prefer per-record WARC-Payload-Digest values (present in the CDX) and/or the WACZ datapackage-digest, or explicitly record "no whole-file fixity" for streamed WACZs. Update `verify` and the manifest accordingly, and make sure a WACZ indexed by streaming vs by download is still verifiable in some meaningful way. Decision task first, then implementation.
notes: Fixity for streamed sources. verify ALREADY skips remote (URL) sources ('REMOTE - skipped, not re-fetched'), so streaming doesn't regress it. .2 does the minimal handling: streamed entry stores empty sha256 + file_size from HTTP Content-Length. This .4 is the RICHER model: record & check the WACZ's own per-resource integrity from datapackage.json / datapackage-digest.json (already read for metadata) so streamed WACZs are verifiable without re-download; update verify to report/check it.

title: Benchmark streaming vs download; when-to-stream heuristic
description: Benchmark streaming vs full-download across the two axes that matter: media-heavy vs text-heavy WACZs, and remote vs local. Report bytes transferred, request count, and wall-clock. From the CDX we can cheaply compute the wanted-record byte fraction (sum of html/text/pdf lengths / total) — use it to document (and maybe auto-pick) when streaming wins. Deliver a short guidance note and, if warranted, a heuristic default for --stream on remote sources. Measure-first input for the opt-in mode's defaults.

title: Docs: streaming vs download modes and trade-offs
description: Document the two indexing modes (download vs stream), the trade-off (bandwidth/disk vs request count/latency; media-heavy vs text-heavy), the fixity consequence for streamed sources, and the TMPDIR/temp-space behavior of the download path. Update README (Remote WACZ files section) and DESIGN.md (Indexing Pipeline).
close_reason: README + DESIGN updated for streaming vs download modes and trade-offs

title: Parallel CDX-guided record fetch (concurrent range requests)
description:
Streaming a remote WACZ currently fetches page records serially through a single Read+Seek HttpRangeReader cursor: fetch one record's range, block on the full round trip, gunzip+extract, then fetch the next. Measured on a 24.7 GB SUCHO WACZ (S3 eu-central-1, from the US) this runs at ~2 pages/sec.

Diagnosis: this is I/O bound but LATENCY-bound, not bandwidth-bound. Each page record is a separate small HTTP range GET (a single gzipped WARC record, tens of KB); the payloads are tiny and the downlink sits mostly idle while we wait ~100-150ms per transatlantic round trip. Throughput ~= 1/latency, not bytes/bandwidth. That is precisely the regime where concurrency helps: N requests in flight ~= N x throughput until another resource saturates.

Why it is a clean fit here: the CDX already gives (offset, length) for every wanted record up front, so the per-record fetches are INDEPENDENT - they do not need the shared Read+Seek cursor at all. Read the zip central directory + WARC data-starts once serially (cheap), then fan out the thousands of record fetches. rustyweb already uses rayon for the local scan path (index_wacz), so mirror that: parallel fetch + gunzip + record_to_raw into RawRecords, then feed the existing (serial) merge/index step behind the tantivy writer mutex.

Sketch: in collect_page_records_via_cdx, after computing the wanted CDX entries + starts, replace the serial for-loop with a bounded-concurrency parallel map issuing independent range GETs. Options: rayon par_iter with a shared ureq::Agent (Send+Sync, pools connections) doing HttpFetch::fetch(start, len) per record; or an async pool (reqwest) if we want finer control. Keep progress reporting (IndexProgress.set_records) thread-safe (atomic counter). Preserve ordering-independence: the merge is by URL so fetch order does not matter.

Expected win: ~5-15x wall-clock for latency-bound remotes (e.g. ~2/sec -> tens/sec). Ceiling shifts to (a) downlink bandwidth (page records are a small slice of the 24 GB; media we skip is the bulk, so this bites late), (b) CPU for gunzip + HTML text extraction + PDF extraction + tantivy indexing (the real ceiling at high concurrency; also parallelizable), or (c) S3 throttling (unlikely at sane concurrency, e.g. 16-32 in flight).

Caveats / testing surface: pick a sane default concurrency (config or fixed ~16-32) and consider a flag; be gentle to flaky hosts (--download stays the robust escape hatch); ensure errors on one record do not abort the whole run (already warn-and-skip); keep the tail-cache path correct if any shared reader remains for the central-dir/data-starts phase; add a test asserting parity of indexed pages between serial and parallel paths. Coalescing adjacent records (when text clusters in the WARC) is a possible complementary optimization but orthogonal.

Not part of the tail-cache + progress-bar PR (that PR keeps the serial fetch). This is a follow-up behavior change with its own testing surface.
close_reason: Delivered in PR #33: concurrent CDX-guided record fetch over RangeFetch (HttpFetch/FileFetch) on a dedicated pool with --concurrency (default 4 remote / CPU count local); scan-vs-CDX parity test runs through the parallel path.

comments.author: edsu
comments.text: Code review (PR #7) surfaced a related redundancy to fold in here: the CDX-guided read path makes several separate passes over the ZIP central directory/entries — the *_warcs_streamable pre-check calls warcs_stored, then collect_page_records_via_cdx calls ensure_warcs_stored + warc_data_starts + find_warcinfo_streaming, each iterating all entries via by_index (a range fetch per entry for remote). When restructuring for parallel fetch, consolidate into a single pass yielding storedness + per-WARC data_start + warcinfo together. The tail-cache absorbs most of the cost today, so it's not urgent on its own.

title: Portable home directory layout (--home)
description:
Make an archive + its index a single portable folder that can move to another disk, mount point, or machine.

LAYOUT
  <home>/            (default: current directory; --home to override)
  ├── archive/       WACZ files
  └── index/         Tantivy index + collections.json

DESIGN
- One flag --home (default '.') on every subcommand; archive_dir = <home>/archive and index_dir = <home>/index are derived siblings. --home replaces --index-dir as the primary knob (pure --home; overrides can come later if needed).
- Storage: a local WACZ's source is stored RELATIVE to home (e.g. 'archive/docnow.wacz'); that is also what we display. Resolve with home.join(source) to open. Files outside home fall back to an absolute path. Remote http(s) sources still store their URL.
- collection_id derives from the (portable) relative source string, so IDs are stable across moves.
- Downloads (remote URL index, and the coming Browsertrix importer) land in <home>/archive/.
- Back-compat: old manifests with absolute paths still resolve (absolute path used as-is).
- Bonus: 'rustyweb index' with no path indexes everything under <home>/archive/.
- Portability test: index, move the whole home dir, serve/resolve still works.

Reindex is acceptable (single user). Touches CLI (all commands), lib signatures (index/serve/verify/search-url take home and derive/resolve), and collections (relative<->absolute resolution).
close_reason: Portable home-directory layout shipped.

Add resolution (home.join(rel)) wherever a File source is opened: serve_file, search_cdx, verify, re-reads. Derive collection_id from the relative string. Old absolute-path manifests still resolve.

title: Group collection members by site on the collection detail page
description:
On the collection detail page, group member crawls by site instead of showing every crawl as its own tile.

Context: the collection detail page now shows a grid of member crawls, each with its representative image (rustyweb-crawl-thumbnail-s1ff). A collection often contains repeat crawls of the SAME site over time, which share the same og:image, so the grid can look repetitive (e.g. 10 near-identical tiles for one site crawled 10 times).

Idea: collapse members by site (registrable domain, the same `site` value the Site facet uses) into one tile per site, with:
- a representative image (any member's thumbnail for that site),
- a "crawled N times" badge / capture date range,
- expand to the individual crawls (or link to a site-scoped view / search).

Considerations:
- Grouping key: registrable domain (site) vs exact host (domain) vs seed URL. Site (eTLD+1) is probably right, matching the Site facet.
- A collection can legitimately mix many one-off sites (no repeats) — in that case grouping is a no-op and the flat grid is fine, so only group where it reduces clutter.
- Ordering: by most-recent capture, or by crawl count.
- Interaction: clicking a site tile could go to a search scoped to collection + site (collection:<slug> site:<host>), which already works, or to an expanded member list.
- Ties into the two-level collection model and could inform a future "site" as a first-class grouping level.

v1 (flat per-crawl grid) shipped in s1ff; this is the refinement. Lower priority — do if the repetition is noticeable in practice.

title: Per-page og:image gallery on the crawl detail page
description:
Collect the per-page og:image for every page in a crawl (not just the main page) and show a small gallery of those distinct images on the crawl detail page.

Motivation: a crawl covers many pages, each of which may declare its own OpenGraph image (`<meta property="og:image">`). Those are curated, page-level representative images — unlike arbitrary captured resources (icons/pixels/sprites), so no size/noise filtering is needed. A gallery of them gives a rich, honest visual overview of what a crawl contains, and photographs well.

Builds directly on rustyweb-crawl-thumbnail-s1ff, which already:
- extracts og:image per HTML record (search::extract_html_text -> HtmlText.og_image) — currently only the MAIN page's is used;
- has the fetch -> decode -> downscale -> cache pipeline (thumbnail.rs) and the /thumb serving route + CSS.

Scope for a future v1 (keep bounded):
- During indexing, collect og:image across pages, resolve each against its page URL, and DEDUP by resolved image URL (many pages share one site-wide default og:image, so distinct images are what matter).
- Cap the set per crawl (e.g. <= 12-24 distinct images) to bound index time + disk.
- Cache each as a downscaled thumbnail (e.g. <home>/index/thumbs/<crawl_id>/<n>.jpg) and record the set on the manifest, or a manifest sidecar.
- Serve them (extend the /thumb route to an indexed set, or a per-crawl gallery listing) and render a strip/grid on the crawl detail page. The single representative image (main page og:image) stays as the card/hero.
- Graceful: crawls with no per-page og:images just show the single representative image (or placeholder).

Considerations:
- Dedup + cap ordering: prefer distinct images; order by page order / seed pages first.
- Storage growth: N thumbnails per crawl * many crawls — keep N small; needs a reindex to populate.
- This is the "keep it simple, page-level OpenGraph metadata" version the maintainer wanted — explicitly NOT a dump of all captured image resources (that raw-gallery idea was considered and rejected as noisy/expensive).

Relates to s1ff (done) and rustyweb-collection-group-by-site-mwg1. Priority: future / P3.

title: Finding-aid-style provenance & curator context
description:
Make rustyweb surface much richer provenance and encourage curators to share the scope, rationale, and context of archival collections -- an EAD / finding-aid approach -- at the collection and crawl levels.

Motivation (audit of current state): crawl-level TECHNICAL provenance (software/operator/user-agent/robots/pages/capture-dates) is displayed but every row is conditional, so a WACZ with a thin datapackage.json/warcinfo (e.g. the SUCHO crawls) shows almost nothing. Collection-level CURATORIAL provenance is genuinely thin: the Collection struct has only description + curator. Several fields are parsed-but-dropped (warcinfo isPartOf/hostname/conformsTo; datapackage modified) or not read at all (datapackage keywords/licenses/contributors). DESIGN.md already frames curatorial vs technical provenance (Maemura et al., 'If These Crawls Could Talk') and calls for surfacing it prominently.

Decisions (with maintainer): (1) full EAD-ish field set; (2) Markdown narrative for the notes; (3) FOLD this into the Browsertrix epic rustyweb-15z -- Browsertrix brings collection description/tags/org that will populate these fields, so design and build them together. Adding optional manifest fields is backward-compatible and needs NO reindex; re-reading WACZs for the dropped/unread fields DOES need a reindex.

Sequencing: A) model + CLI + UI (no reindex, no Browsertrix dep); B) crawl-level enrichment (reindex); C) Browsertrix mapping (15z.6 maps Browsertrix metadata onto these fields). Full design recorded in the session plan.

comments.author: edsu
comments.text: When deciding the finding-aid field set, factor in what's available from BOTH Browsertrix (org/collection/tags/description; mapped in rustyweb-15z.6) AND Archive-It (rustyweb-kx53): Archive-It exposes rich collection-level metadata via its Partner API (collection title/description, subjects, access/rights, seeds, crawl dates) — design the fields as the union so both importers map cleanly onto the same model. Also relevant: the WARC→WACZ builder (rustyweb-dlqv) prompts for these same fields when packaging loose WARCs, so the finding-aid model is the shared target for all three ingest paths (hand-built WACZ, Browsertrix, Archive-It).

title: Collection finding-aid fields + CollectionFields update struct
description:
Add optional, EAD-mapped fields to the Collection struct (collections.rs), all serde default + skip_serializing_if so existing collections.json loads unchanged (backward compatible, NO reindex -- manifest is separate from the Tantivy index):
- scope (Markdown) -- scope & content note, the centerpiece (EAD scopecontent)
- creator -- collecting org/person (origination), distinct from curator (who runs rustyweb)
- dates -- curatorial coverage statement (unitdate), distinct from the auto-derived capture range
- rights -- access/use/license note (userestrict)
- subjects (Vec) -- topical keywords (controlaccess); Browsertrix tags map here
- provenance (Markdown) -- custodial/acquisition note (custodhist)
Refactor Manifest::set_collection / ensure_collection to take a CollectionFields all-Option update struct (merge = apply only Some), replacing the positional description/curator args, so the CLI and the Browsertrix importer share ONE merge path. This task is the prerequisite/mapping target for 15z.6.

title: Markdown rendering helper (safe subset, no sanitizer dep)
description: Add a library helper to render curator Markdown (scope/provenance/crawl note) with pulldown-cmark (pure Rust, one dep): treat raw-HTML events as escaped text (no passthrough -> no script injection), validate link/image destinations to http/https/mailto (drop javascript: etc.), neutralize images to alt text; return maud::PreEscaped for the views. Avoids the ammonia/html5ever chain. Unit tests: javascript: link dropped; raw script/HTML escaped not passed through; allowed subset (a/em/strong/ul/li/h*/p/code/blockquote) renders.

title: collection set CLI: curatorial flags
description: Extend the collection set subcommand with --scope, --creator, --dates, --rights, --subject (repeatable -> Vec), --provenance; wire through index::set_collection and the CollectionFields merge (main.rs, index.rs). Depends on findingaid task .1.

title: Finding-aid collection detail layout
description: Restructure the collection detail page (views.rs, server.rs) to read like a finding aid: a prominent About this collection front-matter ABOVE the facets -- scope prose (rendered Markdown) as lead, then a curatorial meta_table (Creator, Coverage dates, Rights, Subjects, Provenance note), visually separated from the existing derived/technical aggregates table (Crawls/Size/Software/Capture dates/Created). Reuse MetaRow/meta_table. Everything conditional (graceful when absent). Pass the Collection (or a small view struct) to views::collection. Depends on .1, .2.

title: Empty-state curator nudges
description: When a collection (or crawl) has no curatorial fields, render a muted prompt inviting the curator to add a scope note (e.g. 'Describe why this was archived: rustyweb collection set ...'). The invitation to share rationale, shown exactly where the gap is. Depends on .4.

title: Per-crawl curator note + crawl set CLI
description: Add an optional note (Markdown) field to the Wacz struct for curator annotations (document absences, per Maemura). Add a crawl set <id> --note CLI (manifest-only edit, no reindex). Surface it on the crawl detail page. Depends on .1, .2.

title: Reindex: store & surface dropped/unread provenance fields
description: Store fields already parsed but dropped: warcinfo is_part_of / hostname / conforms_to and datapackage modified (wire in index_one where warcinfo/meta are in scope). Newly parse datapackage keywords (-> crawl subjects) and licenses (-> rights signal) into the DataPackage/Metadata structs (wacz.rs, via clean()). Add the corresponding conditional rows to the crawl provenance panel. Populates on REINDEX; all conditional so un-reindexed crawls just show less. Files: wacz.rs, warc.rs, index.rs, collections.rs, views.rs, server.rs.

title: DESIGN.md finding-aid section
description: Document the finding-aid model in DESIGN.md: the new collection fields and their EAD analogs, the Markdown-note approach + safe-subset rendering, the curatorial-vs-technical/derived split in the UI, and the reindex note for the crawl-level fields.

title: Read-only API, Memento interop & federation across rustyweb instances
description:
Give rustyweb a simple read-only HTTP API, Memento (RFC 7089) interop, and federation so different rustyweb instances (and other archives) can discover each other's content. Read-only to start.

KEYSTONE -- persistent replay URLs. Today a replayable page is a query-string URL (/replay/viewer?source=/files/{id}&url=&ts=&name=&collection=&collection_id=): instance-local, not persistent, and it redundantly encodes name/collection the server could look up. The minimal identity of a memento is (crawl_id, ts, url), and crawl_id is a stable hash of the source. So the persistent URL becomes:
  /crawl/{crawl_id}/{ts}/{url}   e.g. /crawl/a926c132/20220305013545/https://uinp.gov.ua/
It nests under the existing /crawl/{id} detail page (detail vs replay), is bookmarkable/persistent, aligns with single-crawl replay coherence (no composite mementos), and IS a Memento URI-M. Name/collection breadcrumb are looked up server-side from the manifest. This same URL is what the API returns, Memento advertises, and federated peers link to -- so it is built first.

DISCOVERY SUBSTRATE -- web-archive.txt (Mahoney, Terras, Zeller & Lee, "web-archive.txt: A Well-Known Locations Descriptor for Discovery and Coordination of Web Archives", 2026, v0.1). Rather than invent a rustyweb-specific discovery format, adopt web-archive.txt: a TOML descriptor served at /.well-known/web-archive.txt (RFC 8615 well-known URI, content-type text/plain) AND advertised via a Link rel="service-desc" header on the home page. It declares, in four sections: version (version + last_updated); archive identity (id [2-6 lowercase alnum], name inline table w/ en+alt, established, website, email) + archive.organisation (name/type/location ISO-3166/website) + archive.scope (crawl modalities, legal authority + documentation, RFC-3339 temporal coverage interval, domains, and a collections list as id+name inline tables); api (documentation, rate_limit) + api.memento (timemap/timegate endpoint templates + access) + api.cdx (query template + access); and replay state templates (rewritten/no_toolbar/raw). Endpoint templates use {url} + {datetime} (YYYYMMDDhhmmss) placeholders and MAY use {collection} bound to scope.collections.id.

web-archive.txt becomes rustyweb's standard, decentralised identity + capability document: the thing peers read to discover one another (task .5) and the thing external aggregators/validators read. rustyweb PUBLISHES one (new task .9 -- maps its Manifest + collections + Memento routes + persistent replay URL onto the spec's tables) and CONSUMES peers' descriptors for federation config. Because it is a community spec (not rustyweb-specific), rustyweb can federate at the Memento level with ANY web-archive.txt-publishing archive, not just other rustyweb instances. rustyweb keeps its richer NATIVE JSON API (task .2) for rustyweb-to-rustyweb specifics (live counts, capabilities, resolve); web-archive.txt is the interop lingua franca layered over it. NB: scope.authority / scope.coverage / scope.crawl modalities overlap the finding-aid provenance epic (rustyweb-findingaid) -- the descriptor is a natural machine-readable EXPORT of that curatorial metadata, so design the two together.

THREE LAYERS on top:
1. Read-only JSON API -- beyond /api/search: a NATIVE instance descriptor (name, version, collection/crawl counts, capabilities, instance base), /api/collections, /api/collections/{id}, /api/crawls/{id}, and a URL-resolution endpoint /api/resolve?url= -> [{crawl_id, ts, collection}] (page-level, index-backed). Each API result carries its persistent memento URL + the instance base. (The standard cross-archive identity doc is web-archive.txt, task .9; this native JSON is the richer rustyweb-to-rustyweb surface.)
2. Memento (RFC 7089) -- TimeMap + TimeGate + Memento-Datetime/Link headers, built on /api/resolve. Makes rustyweb a first-class Memento server so existing aggregators (Time Travel, MemGator) federate rustyweb instances at the URL level for free; these endpoints are ALSO advertised in web-archive.txt api.memento (task .9). Honest limitation: rustyweb indexes PAGES (HTML/PDF), not every subresource, so TimeMaps are page-level.
3. Federation -- an instance is configured with peer base URLs; it discovers each peer by fetching the peer's /.well-known/web-archive.txt (identity, scope, api endpoints), then does live FAN-OUT search: query self + peers concurrently (reuse the ureq client + retry/backoff + timeouts; degrade gracefully when a peer is down), merge + dedup + ATTRIBUTE each result to its origin instance so links open the peer's persistent replay URL. (Alternative considered: harvest peer catalogs into a local aggregate index -- scalable + resilient but stale/heavier; fan-out chosen for the no-central-service, self-host ethos.)

SYNERGY with cross-WACZ replay (rustyweb-cross-wacz-replay-dk4): dk4's leading approach is exactly a GET /api/resolve?url= lookup (landed on a page not in this crawl -> which other crawl has it -> re-launch). The resolve endpoint (task .3) is that building block; dk4 depends on it. dk4's temporal-coherence caution applies to choosing which crawl/ts to fall back to.

Decisions baked in (maintainer): URL shape = /crawl/{id}/{ts}/{url}; discovery/identity = web-archive.txt at /.well-known/ (adopt the spec, do not invent our own); federation = live fan-out; Memento = included. Task priorities + deps allow deferring Memento or federation. Read-only throughout; consider CORS for cross-instance browser calls and optional rate-limiting for a public API.

comments.author: edsu
comments.text:
Folded in the web-archive.txt spec: Mahoney, Terras, Zeller & Lee, "web-archive.txt: A Well-Known Locations Descriptor for Discovery and Coordination of Web Archives" (2026, v0.1). PDF: ~/Dropbox/Papers/Mahoney/web_archive_txt__A_Well_Known_Locations_Descriptor_for_Discovery_and_Coordination_of_Web_Archives.pdf

Decision: adopt web-archive.txt as rustyweb's discovery/identity substrate rather than inventing our own format. Concretely: new task .9 PUBLISHES /.well-known/web-archive.txt (+ Link rel=service-desc); .5 CONSUMES peers' descriptors for federation config (works for any web-archive.txt-publishing archive, not just rustyweb); .2 keeps the richer rustyweb-native JSON /api/ (dropped the invented /.well-known/rustyweb); .4 advertises its Memento endpoints via api.memento and the persistent replay URL via replay.*. Key spec facts used: TOML at /.well-known/web-archive.txt, text/plain; four sections version/archive/api/replay; {url}+{datetime}(YYYYMMDDhhmmss) placeholders, optional {collection}; RFC 8615 well-known + RFC 2119 language; a validator + IIPC registry are 'forthcoming' (bootstrap layer for archives without a descriptor). Overlap noted with the finding-aid epic (scope.authority/coverage are a curatorial-metadata export).

title: Persistent structured replay URLs: /crawl/{id}/{ts}/{url}
description:
Replace the query-string replay URL (/replay/viewer?source=&url=&ts=&name=&collection=&collection_id=) with a persistent, bookmarkable path that nests under the existing crawl detail page:
  /crawl/{crawl_id}/{ts}/{*url}
Server route parses the path, looks up crawl_id -> wacz source (viewer_source), and renders the viewer configured for (source, url, ts). Name + collection breadcrumb are derived server-side from the manifest (no longer in the URL). Update every internal link builder to emit the new form: search-results rows, crawl-detail replay button + seed-page links, collection viewer_source. Adjust viewer.html to read its config from the path (or server-injected values) instead of query params.
Considerations: the archived URL keeps its scheme in the path (https://...), so handle the // wrinkle (pywb-style) via an axum wildcard capture + normalization; preserve the original URL's own query string. Optionally 302-redirect the old /replay/viewer?... form (nothing external links to it yet). This URL is the Memento URI-M and the link target for the API + federation, so it is the foundation task. Files: server.rs (router + handlers), views.rs, static/replay/viewer.html, DESIGN.md routes table.

title: Read-only JSON API: instance descriptor, collections, crawls
description:
Extend the read-only rustyweb-NATIVE JSON API beyond /api/search:
- Native instance descriptor at /api/: instance name, rustyweb + API version, collection/crawl counts, capabilities (search, resolve, memento, federation), and the instance base URL. The richer rustyweb-to-rustyweb document (live counts + capabilities).
- /api/collections -> list (id, name, description, counts, date range).
- /api/collections/{id} -> collection detail + members (crawl id/name/dates).
- /api/crawls/{id} -> crawl metadata (provenance, seed pages, capture range, source, thumbnail).
Each result/object carries its persistent replay/memento URL (from task .1) and the instance base. Consider CORS (Access-Control-Allow-Origin) for cross-instance browser calls and stable, documented JSON shapes.

NOTE: the STANDARD, cross-archive identity/capability document is web-archive.txt at /.well-known/web-archive.txt (task .9), NOT a rustyweb-specific /.well-known/rustyweb (dropped -- do not invent our own). This native JSON API is the deeper rustyweb-to-rustyweb surface layered under that standard descriptor: /api/ gives live counts + capabilities that the static TOML descriptor does not. Peers identify each other via web-archive.txt (task .5); rustyweb peers can then also read this /api/ for the richer view.

Files: server.rs (routes + handlers), reuse Manifest + search. Depends on .1.

title: URL-resolution endpoint: GET /api/resolve?url=
description: Add GET /api/resolve?url=<original-url> -> JSON list of captures across crawls: [{crawl_id, ts, collection, memento_url}] (optionally &datetime= to sort by closeness). Index-backed exact-URL lookup over the Tantivy url field (page-level; not subresources). This is the shared building block for BOTH Memento (task .4, a TimeMap is this data) AND cross-WACZ replay resolution (rustyweb-cross-wacz-replay-dk4 approach 1: on a miss, find which other crawl has the URL and re-launch). Return closest/most-recent ordering options. Files: search.rs (a resolve query), server.rs (route). Depends on .1.

title: Memento (RFC 7089): TimeMap, TimeGate, Link/Memento-Datetime headers
description:
Expose rustyweb as a Memento server, built on the resolve endpoint (.3):
- TimeMap: GET /timemap/link/{url} (application/link-format) and /timemap/json/{url} -> all captures (URI-Ms = /crawl/{id}/{ts}/{url}) of a URL with their Memento-Datetimes.
- TimeGate: GET /timegate/{url} honoring Accept-Datetime -> 302 to the closest memento URI-M.
- Add Memento-Datetime + Link (rel=original/timemap/timegate/memento) headers on replay responses.
Enables existing aggregators (Time Travel, MemGator) to federate rustyweb instances at the URL level for free.

ADVERTISE these endpoints in web-archive.txt api.memento (task .9): timemap.endpoint + timegate.endpoint as URI templates with {url}/{datetime} placeholders (e.g. timegate = { endpoint = "https://host/timegate/{url}", access = "online" }), so the spec's validator/registry and any web-archive.txt-aware aggregator can find them without prior knowledge. The persistent replay URL maps to replay.rewritten (and a no-toolbar variant to replay.no_toolbar) in the same descriptor.

Honest limitation to document: page-level coverage only (rustyweb indexes pages, not every subresource). Temporal coherence: each URI-M is single-crawl-scoped and coherent. Files: server.rs (routes + headers), search.rs. Depends on .3.

title: Peer configuration for federation
description:
Let an instance be configured with a list of peer base URLs (read-only). Mechanism options to decide: a peers.json under <home> (portable with the home dir) vs a serve --peer flag vs both.

DISCOVER + VALIDATE each peer by fetching its /.well-known/web-archive.txt descriptor (the web-archive.txt spec, task .9) and parsing the TOML: archive.id + archive.name (identity/attribution label), archive.scope (domains / temporal coverage / collections -- lets fan-out skip peers whose scope cannot match a query), and the api.* / replay templates (how to query + how to build the peer's replay/memento URLs). Handle unreachable/incompatible/no-descriptor peers gracefully. Because web-archive.txt is a community spec, a peer need NOT be a rustyweb instance -- any archive publishing a descriptor with api.memento can be federated at the Memento level (task .8); rustyweb peers additionally expose the native /api/ (task .2) for the richer view, so prefer /.well-known/web-archive.txt for identity and fall back to / enrich with /api/ when the peer is rustyweb.

A minimal parser for the four web-archive.txt sections (version/archive/api/replay) is needed here on the CONSUME side; it is the mirror of the PUBLISH side in task .9 -- share a serde struct + a toml dep between them. Peers are trusted-by-configuration; no writes. Files: a config loader (collections.rs or a new config module) + a web-archive.txt model (shared with .9), main.rs (serve). Foundation for fan-out search (.6).

title: Federated fan-out search across peers
description: Add federated search: query the local index AND fan out to each configured peer's /api/search concurrently, then merge. Reuse the ureq client (retry/backoff already present) via spawn_blocking, with a per-peer timeout and graceful degradation (a down/slow peer yields a warning, not a failure; note which peers were skipped). Merge + dedup results (same url across instances) and ATTRIBUTE each result to its origin instance, so its link opens THAT instance's persistent replay URL (from .1). Expose via /api/search?federate=true (or /api/federated-search) and the HTML search page. Latency = slowest responding peer within the timeout. Files: server.rs (federated handler), search.rs (merge), reuse http client. Depends on .2 (peer API), .5 (peer config), .1 (URLs).

title: Federated-results UI (origin-instance attribution)
description: Surface federated results in the web UI: badge/label each result with its origin instance (this instance vs a named peer), link cross-instance results to the peer's persistent replay URL, and show which peers were queried/skipped. Keep local-only search the default; federation opt-in. Files: views.rs, server.rs, app.css. Depends on .6.

title: Memento aggregation across peers (exploratory)
description: Optionally make rustyweb a Memento AGGREGATOR: a federated TimeMap/TimeGate that queries configured peers' TimeMaps (and its own) and merges them, so a single rustyweb instance can answer who-has-this-URL across the federation. This is the Memento realization of federation at the URL level (complements fan-out SEARCH which covers topical/collection discovery). Evaluate vs just letting an external aggregator (MemGator) point at the instances. Depends on .4 (Memento) and .6 (federation). Lower priority / exploratory.

title: Garbage-collect orphaned crawl thumbnails on reindex
description:
The representative-image thumbnails (rustyweb-crawl-thumbnail-s1ff) are cached at <home>/index/thumbs/<crawl_id>.jpg (+ a <crawl_id>.pinned sidecar for curator-pinned ones). Nothing prunes them: when a crawl is removed from the manifest, or its id changes, its thumbnail + pin marker are left behind as orphaned files. reindex rebuilds the full-text index and regenerates thumbnails but does not sweep the thumbs dir; verify doesn't either.

Add a prune step (e.g. at the end of reindex, or a dedicated maintenance action) that removes thumbs/<id>.(jpg|pinned) for any id no longer present in waczs.json. Careful to key off the manifest ids, and to treat pinned thumbnails the same (a removed crawl's pin is also orphaned). Low priority - it's disk cruft, not a correctness issue. Found during the s1ff code review (finding C2).

title: Publish a web-archive.txt descriptor at /.well-known/web-archive.txt
description:
Implement the PUBLISH side of the web-archive.txt spec (Mahoney et al. 2026, v0.1): serve rustyweb's own machine-readable identity + capability descriptor so peers (task .5), external aggregators, and the spec's validator/registry can discover this instance.

- Serve GET /.well-known/web-archive.txt -> a TOML document, content-type text/plain (application/toml MAY be offered). RFC 8615 well-known path.
- Advertise it with a Link: <...>; rel="service-desc" header on the home page (the archive.website resource).

Map rustyweb's model onto the spec's four sections:
- version: version = "0.1" (spec version), last_updated (date the descriptor/manifest last changed).
- archive: id (short slug -- config/serve flag), name (inline table, en + optional alt), established?, website (public base URL), email?.
- archive.organisation: name/type/location(ISO-3166)/website -- from config (overlaps finding-aid curatorial metadata, rustyweb-findingaid).
- archive.scope: crawl modalities, authority {type, documentation}, coverage (RFC-3339 interval, derivable from the index min/max capture dates), domains (derivable from indexed hosts), and collections = the manifest collections as {id, name} inline tables.
- api / api.memento / api.cdx: documentation URL, rate_limit; memento timemap/timegate endpoint templates ({url}/{datetime}) from task .4; api.cdx only if/when rustyweb exposes a CDX endpoint (omit otherwise -- unsupported capabilities SHOULD be omitted).
- replay: rewritten (+ optional no_toolbar/raw) endpoint templates for the persistent replay URL (task .1), with {url}/{datetime} and optional {collection}.

Config-driven identity fields (id/name/org/authority/etc.) have no home in the WACZ data -- add a small instance-config source (serve flags or a config file under <home>) and reuse it for .5 peer model. Share the serde struct + toml dependency with the CONSUME-side parser in task .5. Emit only known-good fields; keep it valid against the spec (required fields present, URLs absolute w/ scheme). Depends on .1 (replay templates); best done with .4 (memento endpoints to advertise). Files: server.rs (well-known route + Link header), a web-archive.txt model module (shared with .5), main.rs/config, DESIGN.md + README routes table. Cross-links: rustyweb-findingaid (scope/authority/coverage are the curatorial-metadata export).

title: Streaming import + refreshable-remote replay for Browsertrix (index-only footprint)
description:
Optional alternative to the always-download flow (15z.3), for self-hosters who want an INDEX-ONLY footprint: stream-index a Browsertrix WACZ from its presigned replay.json URL WITHOUT downloading, and keep replay working despite ~48h presigned-URL expiry.

Supersedes the naive closed 15z.4 (which indexed the presigned URL as a plain Url source): that is not durable because a baked-in signed URL dies in ~48h and replay breaks. The fix is a REFRESHABLE-REMOTE source that re-signs on demand.

Design:
- New Source variant storing the STABLE identity (host + org id + item/crawl id + resource name/hash), not a fixed signed URL.
- Index: resolve a fresh presigned URL, stream-index in place (download=false) — reuses the existing Url/streaming path + http_range range GETs.
- Replay: make viewer_source point at /files/{id} for this source (as for local files); serve_file re-resolves a fresh presigned URL and 302-redirects to it (fits the existing remote-redirect pattern at server.rs ~706). Cache the signed URL until near expiry so we do not re-auth per range request.
- serve needs Browsertrix credentials/config to re-sign (env or a config file under <home>). This is the real operational cost: fine for a self-hoster indexing their own org; a bigger decision for a public/shared instance. Strictly opt-in; download-for-durability (15z.3) stays the default.
- Handle refresh failures gracefully (creds rotated / item deleted / Browsertrix down) with a clear replay-time error.

Contrast with stable remote URLs (e.g. SUCHO on S3): those already stream-index AND remote-replay today via a plain Url source with no refresh needed — this task is only about the EXPIRING-URL case. Depends conceptually on 15z.1 (client resolves resources). Files: collections.rs (Source), index.rs, server.rs (serve_file/viewer_source), main.rs/config, DESIGN.md.

title: Build a WACZ from WARC files (with metadata) and index it
description:
Let a user point rustyweb at one or more WARC files (.warc/.warc.gz); rustyweb PACKAGES them into a WACZ (with metadata) under <home>/archive and then indexes it through the normal pipeline. This is the "I have WARCs, not WACZs" on-ramp, and the reusable building block for the Archive-It importer (which downloads WARCs, not WACZs).

Flow:
- Input: WARC file paths (or a dir/glob). CLI (e.g. `rustyweb wacz build <warcs> [--title ... --description ... --creator ...]`) or fold into `index` when given .warc inputs.
- Metadata: take from flags; for anything missing, PROMPT interactively (title, description, creator/curator, subjects, rights, dates — align with the finding-aid model, rustyweb-findingaid-aoc3). Non-interactive/CI: require flags or use sensible defaults.
- Build: generate the WACZ zip = datapackage.json (name/title/description/created + our finding-aid fields where present), a CDX index, and pages.jsonl (seed/discovered pages) as available. Reuse warc.rs (record iteration) + existing CDX handling to compute offsets.
- IMPORTANT constraint: store the WARCs in the zip UNCOMPRESSED (STORED, not deflated) so CDX offsets map to byte positions — rustyweb’s CDX-guided streaming/replay depends on this (a deflated WACZ is already rejected; see index tests). 
- Then index the built WACZ as a durable local File source.

Non-trivial: this is WACZ *creation* (rustyweb currently only reads WACZ). Consider whether to shell out to py-wacz vs. implement natively in Rust (prefer native, no runtime dep, reuse warc.rs/wacz.rs). 

Sequencing: start AFTER the Browsertrix importer (rustyweb-15z) lands. Files: new builder module (wacz write), warc.rs, index.rs, main.rs (CLI + prompts), DESIGN.md.

title: Import from an Archive-It account
description:
An importer for Archive-It (the Internet Archive’s subscription web-archiving service), analogous to the Browsertrix importer (rustyweb-15z) but for Archive-It collections.

Behaviour:
- Authenticate to an Archive-It account and list content. Import EITHER specific collections/crawls OR everything marked ready/available.
- Archive-It does not (generally) expose WACZ files — it serves WARCs, primarily via WASAPI (the Web Archiving Systems API: e.g. GET /wasapi/v1/webdata returns WARC file records with download locations + checksums, filterable by collection/crawl/date). Verify exact endpoints + auth when building.
- So: download the WARC files for the selected content, then BUILD a WACZ from them via rustyweb-dlqv (the WARC→WACZ builder) and index it — reusing that feature rather than duplicating packaging.
- Map Archive-It metadata (collection name/description, crawl dates, seeds, subjects, access/rights) from its Partner API into rustyweb’s finding-aid Collection/crawl fields (rustyweb-findingaid-aoc3), the same way the Browsertrix importer maps its metadata (rustyweb-15z.6).
- Provenance + incremental sync (skip already-imported WARC sets), mirroring rustyweb-15z.5.

Sequencing: AFTER the Browsertrix importer (rustyweb-15z) lands, and depends on the WARC→WACZ builder (rustyweb-dlqv). Will be broken into tasks (API client, CLI/auth, download+build+index flow, metadata mapping, incremental sync, docs/tests) when scheduled.
